segments.s664-g409: تو خاقان‌نژادی is a nominal/predicative phrase meaning “you are of khaghan-birth” with the copula omitted (a common poetic/colloquial feature). نه introduces a negation of the following origin phrase: نه از کیقباد = “not (from) Kay Qobad,” where از marks origin/descent.
segments.s664-g410: که here links/explains the preceding clause (’that/for’ or ‘and indeed’); the clause that follows is a standard past-tense Persian clause with subject-before-object and the verb نهاد (past of نهادن) final. کسری is the subject, تو را uses the definite object marker را to mark the person affected, تاج بر سر is the thing placed and its location, and نهاد means ‘placed/put’ (past).
segments.s664-g297: بدانست is a past-tense verb meaning 'realized/perceived' placed before the subject (poetic word order: Verb + Subject). بهرام آذرمهان is a compound proper name (subject) following the verb; no additional case marking is required in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s664-g298: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'); آن is a demonstrative 'that' pointing to the thing in question; پرسش is the noun 'question'; شهریار جهان is a genitival phrase 'the sovereign (king) of the world' with جهان qualifying شهریار. The whole segment forms the content clause of 'realized that ...'.
segments.s664-g299: چگونست is a fused question 'how is it' (چگون + ست, archaic/poetic copula). و simply coordinates two questions. آن را uses آن (that) plus the object marker را to mark the thing being asked about. پی و بیخ is a paired noun phrase 'trace/foot (پی) and root (بیخ)' meaning 'origin/essence'. چی ست is the interrogative 'what is'. The word order is typical of classical Persian questions, with the copula ست at the end.
segments.s664-g300: کزان = 'from which' (ک + ز + ان compressed) introduces the source; بیخ 'root' is the source named; او را marks 'him' (the person to be lamented) as definite object; بباید is a modal verb 'it is necessary/one must' and is followed by گریست, here functioning as the action 'to weep/lament' (a quasi-infinitival or subjunctive use). Together they say 'from which root one must lament him.'
segments.s664-g301: سرانجام is an adverb 'in the end/ultimately'; جز is the preposition/particle 'except'; دخمه بی‌کفن is the noun phrase 'an unshrouded tomb/sepulchre' (دخمه = tomb, بی‌کفن = without shroud). The phrase sets up the sole remainder/ outcome.
segments.s664-g302: نیابد is the negative of یابد (he/one does not find or will not find) here used in the present/future sense. ازین is از + این ('from this'); مهتر انجمن is a nominative/construct phrase 'this distinguished/chief of the assembly' or 'this assembly's chief' (مهتر = notable/chief, انجمن = assembly). Word order places the verb before the source phrase, as is common in elevated/poetic diction.
segments.s664-g303: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'gave such an answer' (چنین = thus; داد = gave; پاسخ = answer). که here introduces the direct speech that follows; ای شاه راد is a vocative 'O king, O generous one' (ای = vocative particle, شاه = king, راد = epithet 'generous/noble').
segments.s664-g304: ز is the preposition 'from/of' (poetic variant of از); سیمای is 'the appearance/face of' with the ezafe built in; برزین is the proper name Barzin (the possessor of the face); مکن is the negative imperative 'do not do'; ایچ is an emphatic negator 'at all/not at all'; یاد is 'remembrance/mention' used as the object of مکن ('do not call to mind/mention'). The sequence literally says 'from Barzin's visage do not at all make remembrance.'
segments.s664-g281: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. پیدا شد is a predicative compound: پیدا (visible/appeared) with شد as the past auxiliary/copula, so 'was/appeared'. آن چادر is the noun phrase 'that tent' and عاج گون is a postpositive adjectival compound (عاج‑گون = 'ivory‑colored/ivory‑like') modifying چادر.
segments.s664-g282: خور is the subject here (poetic use = 'the sun' or a sun‑like radiance). The phrase از بخش دوپیکر is a source phrase ('from/ out of the بخش (part/section) described as دوپیکر, “two‑bodied/dual‑formed”'). آمد برون is the verbal phrase 'came forth' — classical order places آمد before the adverb برون (rather than modern برون آمد).
segments.s664-g283: جهاندار is the subject 'the world‑ruler/sovereign'. بنشست is the past tense verb 'sat down' (بن‑ prefix + نشستن). بر تخت عاج is a prepositional phrase 'upon an ivory throne' (تخت = throne; عاج = 'ivory' modifying تخت). Word order is subject + verb + locative phrase.
segments.s664-g284: بیاویختند is a past plural verb 'they hung/affixed' (بی‑ prefix appears in classical verb forms). آن precedes the noun phrase as a demonstrative 'that'. بهاگیر modifies تاج; here بهاگیر functions as an adjectival/epithet applied to تاج (literally 'value‑taking'/'toll‑taking', i.e. a costly or tribute‑taking crown). The verb's object follows in classical order.
segments.s664-g285: بزرگان ایران is a genitive/noun phrase 'the great ones of Iran' (modifier after head). بران is an archaic locative/adverbial (here 'on/at that'), and بارگاه is the place 'the royal court/palace'. The phrase places the great men at that court.
segments.s664-g286: شدند انجمن is a classical/periphrastic expression meaning 'they assembled / became an assembly' (شدند + noun = came into the state of). تا introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'until', and بیامد سپاه is the clause 'the army came' (بیامد is past 'came', سپاه is subject 'army').
segments.s664-g287: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. در here functions as 'door / opening' (classical usage) so ز در پرده = 'from the curtain's opening/door' or 'from behind the curtain'. برداشت is the past verb 'lifted / removed'. سالار is the agent 'the commander/chief' and بار is the object 'load/burden' — word order is somewhat inverted (prepositional phrase before verb, with subject following the verb), typical in poetry.
segments.s664-g288: برفتند is a past plural 'they went/advanced' (classical form). یکسر is an adverb 'all at once / altogether'. بر شهریار is a prepositional phrase 'toward the king/sovereign' (بر used with a motion/result sense). The whole segment means they moved together toward the king.
segments.s664-g305: This is a subordinate nominal clause introduced by که (here = 'that' / 'for' / 'because') stating cause/origin: 'that the ruin of Iran's city is from him.' ویرانی is the verbal noun 'destruction' used as subject, شهر ایران is a genitival phrase 'the city of Iran', and ازوست is an older/poetic fused form meaning 'is from him' (از + او + است contracted). Word order is typical Persian (subject — modifier — predicate) with the copular idea carried by the single fused / predicate word ازوست.
segments.s664-g306: Another clause introduced by که ('that'), describing the same referent: a poetic comparative image. مه is used adjectivally/epithetically here (sense 'great' / 'mickle') modifying مغز ('mind/brain'), بادش is a possessive structure 'his wind/breath/temper', بتن is the fused/prepositional notion 'به تن' = 'on the body', بر is the preposition 'on/upon', مه (here as a simile) = 'moon', and پوست = 'skin'. The phrase assembles as a simile: 'his great mind and his breath lie upon the body like the moon upon the skin.' Note the compacted, archaic poetic order and fused forms.
segments.s664-g307: نگوید here functions as a verbal statement about speech (in this poetic register it conveys 'they said' / 'they did not speak (other)') followed by the object سخن ('words/speech'). جز introduces the exception 'except', همه 'all/every', and بتری is comparative/adverbial 'worse' — together: 'they uttered nothing except ever-worsening words.' Note the inverted/elliptical order common in poetry.
segments.s664-g308: This short clause continues the idea: بر آن بتری = 'upon that worse' (بر = 'on/upon', آن = 'that', بتری = 'worse'). بر کند داوری is a light-verb construction where کند (from کردن, 'to do/make') combines with the noun داوری 'judgment' to mean 'he rendered/judged' or 'he passed judgment.' The order puts the object/ground (بر آن بتری) before the verbal phrase, a poetic fronting for emphasis.
segments.s664-g309: چو here = 'when' (subordinating conjunction) introducing a temporal clause. سیمای برزین is the name (سیما with the epithet/locator برزین) in the nominative/subject position, شنید is the past tense 'heard', and این سخن 'these/this words' is the direct object. Word order is the typical classical Persian sequence: temporal marker — subject — verb — object.
segments.s664-g310: بدو گفت is a short clause meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said'); this is followed by a direct address 표 'کای' = vocative 'O'. نیک یار کهن is a three-word vocative phrase 'good (نیک) friend/companion (یار) old/ancient (کهن)', with adjectives following the noun as is common in Persian poetic diction.
segments.s664-g311: به بد بر تن من گوایی مده is an imperative prohibition: به بد = 'with ill/badly', بر تن من = 'on my body' (بر + تن + من), گوایی = 'appearance/semblance' or 'seeming', and مده is the negative imperative 'do not give' (مده = مده). Literally: 'Do not give a bad semblance to my body.' The order places the object/phrase before the verb, as is idiomatic in poetry.
segments.s664-g312: چنین دیو را آشنایی مده is a parallel prohibition: چنین = 'such/like this', دیو را = 'the demon' (را marks the definite object), آشنایی = 'acquaintance/familiarity', and مده = 'do not give' (negative imperative). Together: 'Do not make acquaintance with such a demon' or 'do not bring me into acquaintance with such a demon.'
segments.s664-g241: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause; بشنید is simple past 3rd-person 'he heard'. گریان is an adjectival/participial predicate 'weeping/tearful', and بشد (شد + past) means 'became'. استوار is another predicate 'steady/firm'; together گریان بشد استوار means he 'became (both) tearful and steadfast' — Classical Persian often strings predicates with شد to mean 'became X and Y'.
segments.s664-g242: بیاورد here functions as the narrative perfective/past 'he brought/uttered' (classical usage of بیاورد to report a completed action). پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply'. بر + شهریار means 'to/toward the king' (بر = toward/to, شهریار = king).
segments.s664-g243: سپهبد is the subject (the spahbed, a high-ranking commander). پشیمان شد is a predicative compound 'became regretful' (پشیمان + شد = 'became sorry'). از کار اوی is a prepositional phrase 'because of / about his deed' with اوی as an enclitic meaning 'his'.
segments.s664-g244: بپیچید is past 3rd-person 'he turned/withdrew' (to turn away). ازان is the demonstrative 'from that / from it' referring back to the mentioned matter. راست‌گفتار اوی = 'his truthful/straightforward speech' (راست‌گفتار = truthful speech; اوی = his), the object of ازان.
segments.s664-g245: مر is an archaic dative/experiencer 'to me' (marks the one affected). آن درد را is the definite object 'that pain' (را marks definiteness). راه چاره is a noun phrase 'a way/means of remedy', and ندید is past negative 'he did not see' — together: 'to me he saw no way of remedying that pain.'
segments.s664-g246: بسی functions as an intensifier 'much/very' (classical usage). باد سرد is literal 'cold wind' used figuratively for a deep cold sigh; از جگر literally 'from the liver' = 'from the heart/seat of feeling' (jigar = seat of feeling). برکشید is past 'he drew up/raised' — idiomatically 'he drew a deep cold sigh from his heart.'
segments.s664-g247: بمرد is an optative/archaic formula often used in epic narration to announce death (literally 'may he die' but used as 'died' in context). آن زمان = 'at that time'. موبد موبدان is an appositional/title phrase 'mobed of mobeds' = chief priest (repetition is honorific/emphatic).
segments.s664-g248: برو here functions as the narrative 'went' (verb before subject is common in verse). زار و گریان شده is a participial phrase 'having become wretched and weeping' describing the people. بخردان is the plural noun 'the wise ones / sages' (subject of the verb), so the sense is 'the sages went away wretched and weeping.'
segments.s664-g249: چنینست is the fused poetic form of چنین است (’such is / it is thus’) serving as the sentence predicate. The order is Subject (کیهان ‘the cosmos’) followed by the predicative phrase همه درد و رنج (‘all [is] pain and suffering’), where همه functions adverbially/quantitatively (‘entirely, all’) and درد و رنج are a fixed noun pair meaning types of suffering.
segments.s664-g250: The correlative construction چه ... و چه ... here means ‘whether ... or ...’. یازی and نازی are poetic/archaic nouns; each is linked by به to a following noun (به تاج / به گنج) and expresses a kind of joy/feeling associated with that object. به marks the relation ‘in/for’ (e.g. ‘pride in the crown’ / ‘delight in treasure’).
segments.s664-g251: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason/explanation (‘that / for’). این روزگار خوشی is the noun phrase ‘this happy time’; بگذرد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive form (poetic) of گذراندن/گذریدن meaning ‘passes’ or ‘will pass’ — the clause states the transience of the present good time.
segments.s664-g252: زمانه is used in the sense of fate/fortune (not merely chronological era) and is the subject of the verb همی‌بشمرد. همی‑بشمرد is the old poetic imperfect/present progressive construction (همی‑ + verb) meaning ‘keeps/counts’ or ‘does regularly’ — نفس را is the definite object with را marking it; literally ‘the age/fate counts the breath’ (i.e. counts each life/breath).
segments.s664-g253: چو شد is the archaic sequence ‘when it became / when it came to pass’. کار دانا reads as ‘the matter/affair of the wise’ or more freely ‘affairs for the wise’; بزاری is an adjectival/predicative form meaning ‘wretched/stricken’ and sits with به سر (‘upon the head’), a common idiom meaning ‘befell’ or ‘came upon’ someone. Word order follows older Persian: temporal clause marker (چو) + verb (شد) + noun phrase + predicate.
segments.s664-g254: همه کشور is the subject noun phrase ‘the whole country/realm’. از marks cause/reason here (‘because of / from’), followed by درد زیر و زبر, a poetic binomial where زیر و زبر means ‘under and over’ or ‘upside down’ — i.e. the realm is afflicted ‘through and through’ by pain/suffering. The phrase expresses total disruption caused by affliction.
segments.s664-g255: This is a noun phrase modifying/introducing the agent: جهاندار (‘world‑ruler, sovereign’) is followed by two adjectival compounds خون‌ریز (‘blood‑showering / bloodthirsty’) and ناسازگار (‘hostile / discordant’). The adjectives are linked by و and describe the ruler’s violent and unfriendly character; word order is typical Persian: head noun + postpositive adjectives.
segments.s664-g256: نکرد is the simple past negative of کردن (‘did not do’); here it negates the transitive verb that follows. ایچ is an emphatic old Persian/vernacular negative meaning ‘nothing / not at all’. یاد is the object ‘memory / remembrance’, and از بد روزگار is a genitival/partitive phrase ‘of the evil/bad times’. The clause therefore states that the ruler left no remembrance of those bad days (i.e. obliterated or caused no memory to remain).
segments.s664-g1: This is a single noun phrase in Persian order: numeric/indefinite modifier 'یکی' ('a certain') + two adjectives 'پیر بد' modifying the head noun 'مرزبان' (marzban, border‑governor). The final word 'هری' is a genitive-place name (Hari/Herat region) placed after the noun it qualifies.
segments.s664-g2: A short predicate/description attached to the preceding noun phrase. 'پسندیده' is a predicative adjective 'esteemed/approved'; 'و' links the next phrase; 'دیده' is a past‑participle meaning 'having seen' and is followed by the oblique preposition 'از' + 'هر' (every) + 'دری' (here 'sea/region'), giving 'having seen every land/sea'.
segments.s664-g3: This clause is a small copular clause in inverted order: 'جهان‌دیده‌ای' (an experienced / well‑travelled one) is predicative; 'نام او بود ماخ' literally 'his name was Makh' places the verb 'بود' before the name for poetic inversion. Here 'ماخ' functions as the proper name in predication.
segments.s664-g4: A verbless series of descriptors: 'سخن‌دان' (eloquent speaker) is coordinated with a list introduced by repeated 'با' meaning 'having/with' ('with farr' — divine/royal glory — 'and with yāl' (mane) 'and shākh' (horn)). The repeated 'با' emphasizes possessions or attributes.
segments.s664-g5: 'بپرسیدمش' is a classical/perfective verbal form: the verbal root 'پرسید' (asked) bears the stylistic prefix 'بـ' and the enclitic object 'ـش' (him) plus the first‑person marker: overall 'I asked him.' 'تا' here introduces an indirect question/purpose and links to 'چه داری بیاد' = 'what do you have in memory' (i.e. 'what do you remember').
segments.s664-g6: A relative clause introduced by 'که' ('who/that') and headed by the oblique preposition 'ز' (poetic 'of/about' or 'concerning'). 'هرمز' is the referent (here the divine/legendary Hormoz), 'بنشست' is the past tense 'he sat', and the locative phrase 'بر تخت داد' means 'upon the throne of Justice' (تخت = throne; داد = justice).
segments.s664-g7: 'چنین گفت' is a conventional formula meaning 'thus said' (it introduces reported speech). 'پیر خراسان' is the speaker's title/name ('the old man of Khorasan'); 'که' here begins the subordinate clause that follows (literal 'that/when/which') and the next word 'شاه' is the subject of that clause.
segments.s664-g8: 'چو' (classical poetic form) means 'when'; 'بنشست' is the past tense 'he sat'; the preposition 'بر' + adjective 'نامور' + noun 'پیشگاه' yields 'upon/before the renowned presence/court' — a temporal/locative clause setting the scene for the quoted speech.
segments.s664-g401: به خط پدر هرمز is a prepositional phrase linking the object to its source: به + خط = 'in/by the handwriting (of)'; پدر هرمز is a genitive (father Hormoz). آن رقعه is the direct object 'that note', followed by the past verb دید ('he saw'). The verb is simple past 3rd singular and the subject is omitted (understood from context).
segments.s664-g402: هراسان شد is a two-word predicate: هراسان (adjective 'frightened') + شد (past of شدن 'became'), indicating a change of state. The conjunction و links a coordinated clause پرنیان برکشید, where پرنیان ('silken robe') is the object and برکشید is the simple past 'raised/lifted' (بر- + کشیدن).
segments.s664-g403: دو چشمش ('his two eyes' = both eyes) is the subject phrase; پر از خون شد is a predicate built with the adjective پر + preposition از + noun خون and شد (became), i.e. 'became full of blood'. The following و joins a verbless coordinate clause روی زرد where the verb 'شد' is omitted by ellipsis and understood to apply to روی ('face') + زرد ('pale').
segments.s664-g404: به بهرام گفت places a prepositional dative (به + name) before the verb گفت ('said', simple past). The following ای جفاپیشه مرد is a vocative: ای introduces the direct address, جفاپیشه is an epithet 'oppressor/cruel', and مرد is 'man' used in address.
segments.s664-g405: چه جستی is a past second‑person interrogative ('what did you seek/what were you trying?'); جستی here uses the sense 'to seek' rather than 'to leap'. ازین (از + ین, an orthographic/informal form of این) introduces the source 'from this', and اندرهمی qualifies رقعه as 'worth a dirham' (a dismissive/low value).
segments.s664-g406: بخواهی is the present/subjunctive second‑person form 'you want/would you like'; it takes the infinitive ربودن ('to steal/snatch') as its object. ز من سر is a prepositional phrase 'my head from me' (ز = 'from'); همی is an emphatic/poetic particle ('indeed/just') attached for force.
segments.s664-g407: بدو گفت is an inverted word order common in poetry: بدو ('to him') + گفت ('said'), with the speaker name following (بهرام). کای ترک‌زاد is a vocative: کای = 'O' and ترک‌زاد 'Turk‑born' addressing the addressee with an epithet.
segments.s664-g408: به خون ریختن is an infinitival/prepositional phrase where به + noun + infinitive conveys means or manner ('by/with shedding blood' — به used as 'by/-ly'). تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that/in order that' and نباشی تو شاد is a negative subjunctive/purpose clause meaning 'you may not be joyful' (نباشی = 'may you not be / you are not').
segments.s664-g145: گزند آیدم is a classical word-order where گزند (harm) is the thing that 'comes' and آیدم is 1st-person inflected present/past style verb meaning 'comes to me' or 'befalls me' (آید + ـم). زین is the fused form of زِ این (= 'from this') introducing the agent/source, and جهاندار مرد is a postpositive noun phrase 'world-ruling man' (جهاندار modifying مرد).
segments.s664-g146: کند is the 3rd-person singular of کردن here used as a lexical verb 'makes/does' (he causes/does). بر من is the prepositional phrase 'against/to me' (بر + من). از خشم gives the cause 'from/because of anger' (classical use of از), and رخساره زرد is the noun + adjective 'countenance pale' (زرد as predicate adjective after رخساره).
segments.s664-g147: هم 'also' introduces the clause; از بهر is a classical prepositional compound meaning 'for/for the sake of' (از + بهر). ایزد‌گشسب is a proper name (the person for whom the role applies) and دبیر is the noun 'secretary' (the role/title). The whole segment reads as 'also for Izad‑Gushasp, (he was) secretary' or 'also as secretary to Izad‑Gushasp'.
segments.s664-g148: دلش is the noun دل 'heart' plus the enclitic ـش 'his', and بود is the past copula 'was'. پیچان is an adjective here meaning 'winding' in the sense 'devious/treacherous'. The conjunction و links the second predicate رخ چون زریر: رخ 'face' + چون 'like' + زریر (a proper name) gives a simile 'his face like Zarir's.'
segments.s664-g149: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a purpose or result clause 'so that' or 'that'. پاک is an adverb here meaning 'entirely/utterly' modifying what follows. خوالیگرش is an obscure compound in the manuscript (treated here as a single noun form); the final ـش may mark possession (his) as part of that token.
segments.s664-g150: به زندان is the prepositional phrase 'into/into the prison' (به + زندان). کشد here functions as the verbal centre of the subordinate clause (classical/poetic subjunctive/imperative sense meaning 'place/lead/put'), and خوردنی‌ها is the plural noun 'the edibles/food' (object). برش in this construction means 'to him' / 'for him' (i.e. food brought to him in prison); the exact lexical shape is irregular in the provided sense list so it is treated as a context-dependent form.
segments.s664-g151: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that/thereupon'. نشست is the past 3rd-person form 'he sat' used idiomatically with از بر to mean 'he alighted/dismounted'. از بر (literally 'from upon') marks the source from which he dismounted, and تازی اسب is a poetic inversion meaning 'the Arabian horse/Arabian steed' (تازی modifying اسب).
segments.s664-g152: بیامد is past 3rd-person 'he came'. به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to near/close to' (به + نزدیک) indicating approach, and ایزدگشسب is the proper name of the person approached. The whole segment means 'he came near to Izad‑Gushasp.'
segments.s664-g321: This is a compressed noun phrase listing title(s) and a personal name: 'موبد موبدان' is a genitive/epithetic stacking meaning 'mobed of the mobeds' (i.e. chief priest), and 'برزمهر' is the proper name Barzmehr. There is no copula; classical Persian often strings titles and names directly. The initial token 'ابا' is not functioning clearly as a modern lexical item here (appears as an isolated vocative/particle or manuscript variation).
segments.s664-g322: 'چو' here means 'like'/'as' and introduces a simile/apposition that links the preceding name to a poetic epithet. 'ایزدگشسب' is a proper name, followed by the demonstrative/article 'آن' and the noun 'مه' (moon) with the adjectival epithet 'خوب‌چهر' (fair‑faced) after it — adjective after noun is normal in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s664-g323: 'بپرسید' is a past‑tense narrative verb 'he asked' (third person). The phrase 'کین تخت شاهنشهی' is the object: 'تخت شاهنشهی' = 'the imperial throne' with 'کین' preceding as a demonstrative/pointing word in classical usage (here treated as part of 'this throne'). Word order places the verb before its object, a common narrative pattern.
segments.s664-g324: 'کرا' is an archaic interrogative meaning 'who/whom'. 'زیبد' is a present/stative verb meaning 'befits' or 'is fitting for' (used impersonally with the throne as the thing to befit someone). The clause then continues 'و کی ست با فرهی' where 'کی' = 'who', 'ست' = copula 'is' (split as separate word), and 'با فرهی' = 'with nobility' (i.e. 'noble' / 'of noble character'); the structure is an older, poetic ordering where the copula can appear separated from the interrogative.
segments.s664-g325: This clause contains a conditional: 'دهم‌گر' = دهم + گر, an older conditional marker meaning 'if I give'. 'به کهتر' = 'to the lesser' and 'به مهتر پسر' = 'to the greater/elder son' — the preposition 'به' is repeated for the two contrasting recipients. 'مهتر پسر' is a noun phrase 'the elder/distinguished son'.
segments.s664-g326: 'که' here is a relative/interrogative 'who/which', and 'باشد' is the present/subjunctive copula meaning 'would/might be' or simply 'is' in classical narrative. 'به شاهی سزاوارتر' is a comparative phrase: 'به شاهی' = 'to kingship', and 'سزاوارتر' is the comparative form 'more deserving/fit' (‑تر marks the comparative).
segments.s664-g327: 'همه یکسر' is an adverbial phrase 'all at once/entirely'; word order places this adverbial before the verbal phrase. 'از جای برخاستیم' is literal 'we rose from (our) places' with 'برخاستیم' being the past plural 'we rose/departed'.
segments.s664-g328: Literal breakdown: 'زبان' here means 'speech/utterance' (not the anatomical tongue) and functions as the instrument or manner of replying; 'پاسخش را' = 'its answer' with the possessive suffix on 'پاسخ' and object marker 'را' marking the direct object; 'بیاراستیم' is a poetic/historical formation from بی‑ + آراستن meaning 'we arranged/prepared' — so the full idea is 'we arranged/poised our speech to answer.'
segments.s664-g153: گرفتند is a past tense active verb (they seized/took). مر is the archaic accusative pronoun 'me' (equivalent to modern مرا) and appears before the rest for emphasis. یکدگر را is the object 'one another' marked by را (definite/object marker). کنار here means 'aside' / 'to the side' and indicates the place to which the taking/seizing occurred.
segments.s664-g154: پر از ... is a phrase meaning 'full of ...' (پر with از). درد is used as 'pain'/'sorrow' (emotional suffering). ومژگان links 'and eyelashes' (مژگان) as a second element; چو introduces a simile 'like', and ابر بهار is the image 'a cloud of spring' (eyelashes compared to spring clouds). The whole clause describes their appearance/state as they were taken aside.
segments.s664-g155: ز here means 'because of' (a poetic preposition). خوی بد شاه is a genitive chain: 'the king's bad disposition/ill temper' (خوی = disposition, بد = bad, شاه = king). چندی modifies سخن and means 'for a while' (duration). سخن = 'talk/speech'.
segments.s664-g156: همی‌رفت is an imperfective/habitual past form meaning 'went on' / 'was going' (continued). تا is the conjunction 'until'. شد is the past of شدن 'became' (here 'came to be' / 'grew'). سخنها is the plural 'words/talks' and کهن means 'old/ancient' (i.e. the talk grew old — it continued so long it became old news).
segments.s664-g157: نهادند is the past tense verb 'they placed/set' (here used transitively). خوان means 'banquet/feast' (an object placed). پیش with the genitive/presence meaning 'before/in the presence of' and is followed by the proper name ایزدگشسب (Izad‑Gushasp), the beneficiary/recipient of the action.
segments.s664-g158: گرفتند پس is 'they then took/ seized' (past tense + temporal 'then'). واژ is an archaic/poetic word here used as a substantive/adjective meaning 'overturned' or 'upended' (the text compresses meaning; it likely refers to items turned over or spoil). و is the conjunction 'and'. برسم بدست is a compound where برسم (in this context) carries the sense of 'reaching/attaining' or 'taking into account' and بدست literally 'in hand' — together implying taking possession/handling. Overall the clause describes that, after placing the feast, they took hold of certain (overturned) things and held them in hand.
segments.s664-g159: پس is the connective 'then'. ایزدگشسب is the subject (proper name). آنچ is a relative/pronominal adverb 'that which' or 'what' and governs اندرز 'advice/counsel'. بود is the past form of 'to be' (was) marking that counsel existed — 'the counsel that there was'.
segments.s664-g160: به + زمزم is 'to Zamzam' (the preposition به marks direction/recipient here; زمزم is treated as a proper noun/place/person in the narrative). همی‌گفت is the habitual/presentive past 'kept saying' or 'said repeatedly'. و is 'and'. موبد is 'mōbed' (a Zoroastrian priest) and شنود is classical present/past-subjunctive form 'he hears/listened' — here it functions as 'the mobed listened'.
segments.s664-g257: میان تنگ is a locative phrase 'in/at the narrow pass/defile' placed before the verbal phrase. خون ریختن is a verbal noun ('the spilling of blood' / 'bloodshed') marked by را as the definite/accusative object of the verb. ببست is a past transitive verb 'he closed / he stopped' placed at the end in classical word order, so the whole clause reads 'he stopped/closed the bloodshed in the narrow pass.'
segments.s664-g258: به + name introduces the indirect object/goal ('to Bahram Azarmehān'). آخت دست is a compressed, somewhat archaic/poetic collocation meaning 'made a motion of the hand / stretched out his hand / laid his hand (on/toward)'; دست is the noun 'hand'. The verb (=آخت) follows the indirect object, so the phrase means 'he put out his hand toward Bahram Azarmehān.'
segments.s664-g259: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; شب تیره‌تر شد = 'the night became darker.' مر is the indirect object 'to me' placed before the direct object (او را). او را is the direct object 'him' and بخواند is the past verb 'he called/summoned.' The clause thus reads 'When the night grew darker, he summoned him to me.'
segments.s664-g260: به پیش خود = 'before/in the presence of himself' (i.e., before him). اندر به زانو نشاند is a fixed poetic sequence: اندر ('in/on') + به زانو ('on the knee / on his lap') + نشاند (past transitive 'he made sit / seated'). The earlier object (او را) is understood as the object of نشاند, so the phrase means 'he seated him on his lap before himself.'
segments.s664-g261: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (bədu = 'to him'); what follows is reported content. خواهی here is a 2nd-person present/future 'you want' or an introductory 'if you wish' followed by the subordinator که and the subjunctive شوی 'you become' (ایمن شوی = 'you may be safe'). The clause therefore functions as 'He said to him: if you wish to be safe...' (classical sequence with که + subjunctive).
segments.s664-g262: نبینی is the negative present/subjunctive form 'you do not see / you shall not see' used to express the desired/resulting outcome. ز is the poetic preposition 'from,' so ز من = 'from me' (i.e., 'experience at my hands'). تیزی و بدخوی are two coordinated nouns 'sharpness / fierceness and ill-temperedness.' Together: 'you will not see from me sharpness and ill temper.'
segments.s664-g263: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; خورشید بر برج روشن شود literally 'the sun becomes bright on the tower' where بر برج is the prepositional phrase 'on the tower' and روشن شود is a passive/intransitive predicate 'is made bright / shines.' The construction is a typical classical order: temporal 'چو' + subject + locative + adjective + auxiliary.
segments.s664-g264: سرکوه is the subject 'the mountain-top.' چون here means 'like' (a simile), so 'سرکوه چون پشت جوشن شود' = 'the mountain-top becomes like the back of a cuirass.' پشت جوشن is a genitive phrase 'the back of the armor/cuirass'; شود is the copular verb 'becomes/is.'
segments.s664-g185: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate time clause; موبد is the subject 'the mobed' (a Zoroastrian priest); بیامد is the past tense 'came' (3rd singular). بهنگام بار is an adverbial phrase 'at the time/occasion of the feast' where بهنگام = 'at the time of' modifies بار ('occasion; feast'). The whole segment = 'when the mobed came at the time of the feast.'
segments.s664-g186: به + نزدیکی forms a locative/motion phrase 'to/toward the vicinity (nearness) of ...'; نامور شهریار is a noun phrase 'the famed/renowned king' with نامور 'renowned' qualifying شهریار 'king'. Word order is preposition + noun phrase: 'to the vicinity of the famed king.'
segments.s664-g187: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = past 'said'); کامروز is a temporal adverb 'today' placed before the subordinate phrase for emphasis. ز ایدر مرو reads as a short command-like locative sequence 'from here to Merv' (ز = 'from/of' with poetic force, ایدر = 'here', مرو is the place-name 'Merv' here). The sequence functions as the destination/time phrase reported in his speech.
segments.s664-g188: که introduces the content clause 'that/for' giving the reason; خوالیگری is a deverbal/abstract noun 'a hankering/thing to desire' (an object or source of desire); یافتستیم is past plural 'we found/obtained' (یافت + -یم for 'we'); نو is a postpositive adjective 'new'. Together: 'that we have found a new khavali-gari (a new object/source of desire).'
segments.s664-g189: چو بنشست موبد is a temporal clause: چو = 'when', بنشست = past 'sat down' (3rd singular), موبد = subject 'the mobed'. نهادند خوان is the main clause 'they placed/set the banquet' (نهادند = past plural 'they placed/set', خوان = 'banquet, table of food'). Note the classical SOV flexibility: temporal clause then main clause.
segments.s664-g190: ز موبد here marks agent/source, 'by/from the mobed'; بپالود is a past-form verb 'purified/cleansed' (classical form with prefix بـ); رنگ is 'color/complexion' and رخان (here) refers to the faces/cheeks or their appearance (an older poetic form). The clause says roughly 'by the mobed the complexions were purified' — agent and action are classical/poetic in order and some nouns are archaic.
segments.s664-g191: بدانست is past 'he knew/he realized'; کان is an archaic/deictic particle ('that'/'indeed'/'so') introducing the reported clause; خوان زمان ویست is a nominal/predicative phrase where خوان = 'the banquet', زمان = 'the time', and ویست (= وی است) = 'it is' — so 'that the banquet is the time'. The clause as a whole: 'he realized that that banquet was the (proper) time.'
segments.s664-g192: همان راستی = 'that very truth' or 'the same truth' (همان = 'that/the same', راستی = 'truth/rightness'); در گمان ویست = 'is in his thought/belief' (در = 'in', گمان = 'belief/opinion', ویست = 'it is'). The whole segment says he held that truth in his mind — an emphatic restatement of his conviction.
segments.s664-g385: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign' (title). صندوق را is the definite direct object: صندوق 'chest/box' plus the object marker را placed after it. برگشاد is the past verb (from برگشودن) 'opened' and completes the clause (S‑O‑V order typical of Persian poetry).
segments.s664-g386: فراوان is an adverb 'abundantly/plentifully' modifying the verbal idea that follows. ز introduces the person whose memory is invoked ('of/about Nushin‑rūvān'). کرد یاد is a light‑verb construction: کرد 'did/made' + noun یاد 'remembrance' = 'remembered/caused remembrance'; the whole literal order is adverb + preposition + name + compound verb.
segments.s664-g387: به صندوق در حقه is a sequence of locative phrases: به (towards/into) + صندوق 'the chest', then در حقه 'in/inside the hidden compartment' (حقه = inner pouch/secret compartment). با مهر 'with a seal' describes what he perceived; دید is the past 3rd‑person verb 'he saw/observed'. Word order is topical locatives before the verb, as is common in poetry.
segments.s664-g388: شتابید is the past 3rd‑person 'he hurried/was hastening'. و is the conjunction 'and'. ز (poetic form of از) here means 'from/therefrom' (often attached as ز + او → زو), linking to what follows. پرنیان برکشید is a verb+object phrase: پرنیان 'silken cloth/robe' (object) + برکشید 'drew up/withdrew' (past), i.e. 'drew forth a silken robe from it.'
segments.s664-g389: نگه کرد is a compound of the noun نگه 'gaze/attention' with the light verb کرد 'did' and here means 'he looked' or 'he fixed his gaze'. پس is an adverb 'then/afterwards'. خط نوشین‌روان is a noun phrase 'the handwriting/script of Nushin‑rūvān' (possessive/genitive relationship implied by juxtaposition).
segments.s664-g390: نبشته is 'inscription/written text'. بران is a fused/poetic locative particle meaning 'upon that' or 'thereon' (linking the inscription to what follows). رقعه پرنیان = 'a note/scrap (رقعه) of silk (پرنیان)' — adjective follows the noun in this poetic ordering, so the phrase reads 'the inscription upon that silken note.'
segments.s664-g391: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'that'. هرمز is the divine name Hormoz (Ahura Mazda) as agent. بده is an archaic/poetic present/past form of 'give' (here 'gave/granted'). سال is 'year/years'. و بر سر دوسال: و 'and'; بر سر is an idiom 'upon the head' = 'for the span of' or 'for'; دوسال 'two years' specifies the duration. Together the clause says Hormoz gave years and (specifically) two years upon his head (i.e. he ruled for two years).
segments.s664-g392: یکی here functions as 'a certain/one' or 'a single' modifying شهریاری 'sovereignty/kingly rule'. بود is the past copula 'was'. بی‌همال is an epithet meaning 'without equal/peer' in the Shahnameh context; it appears here as a qualifying adjective on the sovereignty. The clause states that he was vested with a unique/unmatched sovereignty.
segments.s664-g193: خورش‌ها (the object) is fronted as a topicalized noun phrase; ببردند is a past plural verbal form 'they took' (classical past), and the agent خوالیگران follows the verb — an object–verb–subject (OSV) order allowed in poetry. The verb's -ند ending marks plural third person.
segments.s664-g194: همی‌خورد uses the old poetic prefix همی- to signal continuous/progressive action ('kept eating'); شاه is the subject that follows the verb. از...تا... is a standard prepositional range phrase 'from...to...' specifying extent (از = 'from', تا = 'to/as far as').
segments.s664-g195: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; آن کاسه زهر is the noun phrase 'that bowl/cup of poison'; پیش آورید is a verbal compound where پیش 'forward/in the presence of' combines with آورید (here a past/pluperfect or classical past-plural form 'they brought') to mean 'brought forward/brought before (someone)'.
segments.s664-g196: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه (watch, gaze) + کرد 'did' = 'kept watch/held his gaze'; موبد (the priest) is the subject placed after the verb (poetic order). بدان is be- + آن 'at/onto that', and بنگرید is a classical/perfective form meaning 'looked' or 'regarded' — together 'and he looked at that.'
segments.s664-g197: بران 'at/upon that' introduces the stimulus; بدگمان شد is a two-word verbal phrase 'became suspicious' (بدگمان = suspicious, شد = became — past). دل‌پاک اوی is a nominative appositive 'the pure‑hearted one (he)', with اوی functioning as a third‑person pronoun following the noun.
segments.s664-g198: که introduces the content/explanatory clause 'that/because'; زهرست is ẓahr + -ست ('is poison') — a nominal sentence 'it is poison'; بر خوان تریاک اوی is a prepositional/locative phrase 'on/at the table of opium (of his),' with اوی here functioning as possessive 'his.'
segments.s664-g199: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause; هرمز is the subject (proper name). نگه کرد is again the light‑verb phrase 'fixed/held his gaze', لب را uses را as the definite/direct‑object marker 'his lip' and ببست is simple past 'he closed/sealed' — together 'he closed his lips.'
segments.s664-g200: بران here means 'toward/at that'; کاسه زهر is the noun phrase 'bowl of poison'; یازید is a classical past form meaning 'he stretched forth/opened' (here 'reached out'), and دست 'hand' is the object — 'he reached out his hand to that bowl of poison.' Word order places the object phrase before the verb in poetic style.
segments.s664-g201: بران سان is a fixed comparative phrase: بران ('on/thereon/in that way') + سان ('such/like'), together meaning 'in that same way' or 'just as'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that kings show favor' with شاهان (subject, plural) followed by نوازش کنند, a present subjunctive/habitual form meaning 'show/perform kindness' (کنند used as general/optative or habitual). Word order is topic-comparison → complementizer که → subject → verb.
segments.s664-g202: بران here repeats 'in that same way' (adverbial). بندگان (subjects) + نیز ('also/likewise') marks the parallel; نازش is ناز + -ش (the -ش is a clitic meaning 'to him/her/it' = 'his/her/its' or an object clitic attached to the noun), so نازش کن��د literally 'do his pampering' → 'they show him fondness'; کن��د is the habitual/present plural 'they do'. Word order: adverbial → subject → adverb → verb phrase.
segments.s664-g203: ازان ('from that') is a prepositional phrase pointing back to the previously mentioned bowl; کاسه is the noun 'bowl/cup' and برداشت is a past-tense or perfective verbal form from برداشتن meaning 'took/lifted/removed' (here physical removal). مغز استخوان is a noun phrase 'marrow of the bone' functioning as the direct object of برداشت. Word order is prepositional phrase → object → verb (typical in older/poetic Persian).
segments.s664-g204: بیازید is an archaic/poetic imperative/optative from یاخت/یاز meaning 'open, burst forth' (here a command or exhortation). دست گرامی is a vocative or appositive 'honored one' (literally 'honored hand', a respectful epithet). بخوان is an imperative 'read/recite' (addressing the priest). The clause strings two imperatives/commands: 'Open! (then) call/let the honored one recite.'
segments.s664-g205: به موبد is a dative prepositional phrase 'to the mobed' (mōbed = Zoroastrian priest). چنین گفت literally 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت here functions as the verb 'said' — note the provided sense list treats گفت as a noun, but in the line it is the past-tense verb 'he said'). کای is a vocative particle 'O' used to address someone, followed by پاک‌مغز, an epithet 'pure‑minded' (پاک + مغز).
segments.s664-g206: تو را here uses the object marker را after the pronoun to mark the indirect/beneficiary 'for you'; کردم is the first‑person past 'I made/prepared/did' (کردن used transitively: 'I prepared/made for you'). این لقمه پاک و نغز is the object phrase 'this morsel, pure and delightful' (لقمه = morsel; پاک = pure; نغز = choice/delightful). Word order: indirect object (with را) → verb → demonstrative + noun + adjectives.
segments.s664-g207: دهن بازکن is an imperative 'open your mouth' (دهن = mouth, بازکن = 'open!' imperative). تا introduces a purpose/result clause here meaning 'so that / in order that'; خوری is subjunctive/present-tense hortative 'you may eat' (from خوردن). زین خورش is 'of/from this food/dish' (زین here functions as a deictic 'from this' modifying خورش 'dish'). The structure is command → purpose clause (imperative … تا … subjunctive).
segments.s664-g208: کزین پس combines کزین ('from this' / 'hence from this') + پس ('then/after'), giving 'from this time forth'. چنین باشدت is an optative/formal subjunctive construction: چنین ('thus') + باشدت (archaic form meaning 'may it be / shall it be for you'), so the sense is 'may/shall your ... be thus'. پرورش is the noun 'upbringing/training/development' and is the predicate of the optative. Overall: 'From this onward, may your upbringing be thus' / 'henceforth your upbringing will be like this.'
segments.s664-g81: This is a relative clause introduced by هرآن‌کس که (‘whoever/anyone who’). The particle که links the relative to the verb شد (past of شدن, 'become'). در جهان is an adverbial phrase ('in the world'). شاه‌فش is a predicate/complement after شد (i.e. 'becomes شاه‌فش'); here شاه‌فش is a poetic/compound noun meaning something like 'victim of the king’s blow/royal onslaught'.
segments.s664-g82: سرش is the noun سر ('head') with the third‑person possessive suffix -ش ('his'). گردد is the literary/present subjunctive form of شدن used here with a future/neutral sense ('becomes' / 'will be'). از introduces the agent in a passive-like construction ('by/from'), so از گنج دینارکش names who effects the action: 'by the treasure’s money‑collector.'
segments.s664-g83: سرش را is the definite object (سر + possessive -ش + object marker را). بپیچم is a first‑person form (prefix ب‑ + present/subjunctive stem) used here with volitional/intentional force: 'I will/can twist.' ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of' and کنداوری is a proper name (the reason/agent invoked).
segments.s664-g84: نباید که is an impersonal prohibitive phrase 'it must not be that / one should not'. The verb جوید (present/subjunctive) follows and here means 'seek' or 'strive for'. Classical Persian allows the indefinite subject کسی ('anyone') to appear after the verb, and مهتری is the object meaning 'preeminence/supremacy'—so the clause reads 'it should not be that anyone seeks preeminence.'
segments.s664-g85: چنین است is a copular formula 'thus is' or 'such is'. انجام و آغاز ما is a noun phrase 'our end and beginning' where the possessive pronoun ما follows the nouns (a common classical ordering: noun(s) + ما = 'our ...').
segments.s664-g86: سخن گفتن is a verbal noun/infinitive construction meaning 'speaking' or 'to speak'; فاش functions adjectivally/adverbially ('plainly/openly'). و هم links a second element; راز ما ('our secret') is a noun + possessive pronoun where راز is the object/paired idea. Together the phrase lists two characteristic actions/states: 'speaking frankly and (keeping) our secret.'
segments.s664-g87: درود ('greeting/blessing') is the subject; جهان‌آفرین ('world‑creator') qualifies or apposes it (i.e. 'the Creator of the world'). بر شماست is a prepositional predicate meaning 'is upon you' (بر + شما + the contracted copula ست). This is a conventional benedictory formula.
segments.s664-g88: This is a stacked nominal phrase: خم ('curve/bend') modified by چرخ گردان ('revolving wheel') with زمین ('earth') named and the possessive copula شماست at the end ('is yours'). گردان here is a participial/adjectival form 'revolving,' and the overall order (nouns before the copula) is typical of classical Persian syntax.
segments.s664-g233: بدو گفت = 'he said to him.' بدو is the dative/enclitic phrase 'to him/unto him.' گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (past) introducing direct speech. The following imperatives appear in the reported command: رو (an imperative 'go') is placed before the prepositional phrase پیش هرمزد ('before/unto Hormozd') and is followed by گوی (imperative 'say' or 'speak'), which issues the instruction to address Hormozd.
segments.s664-g234: که introduces the content clause ('that ...'). بختت is the subject 'your fortune' (بخت + enclitic -ت). به + infinitive برگشتن expresses direction/result 'toward returning' and آورد is the simple past 'brought/caused' (3rd sing.). روی ('face') is literal but used figuratively here: 'the face/turning'—so the clause means 'that your fortune has turned toward returning.' Word order places the subject before the verb and the verbal phrase برگشتن آورد together to indicate resulting motion/change.
segments.s664-g235: بدین داوری = 'by/with this judgment' (بدین = به + این, an adverbial 'by this'); داوری is 'judgment' or 'verdict.' نزد داور شویم uses نزد + noun 'in the presence of the judge,' and شویم is the 1st-person-plural subjunctive/optative of شدن used here as a hortative 'let us go/present ourselves' or 'we shall present ourselves.' The clause expresses proposal/action together: 'let us present ourselves before the judge with this judgment.'
segments.s664-g236: به جایی که = 'to a place where' (به + جایی, followed by relative/result clause introduced by که). هر دو برابر شویم: هر here with دو forms the sense 'both/each of the two,' برابر 'equal,' and شویم (1pl subjunctive) yields 'we may be made equal' or 'we shall be equal.' The whole phrase expresses movement to a place where both parties will stand equal.
segments.s664-g237: ازین پس = 'from now on' or 'henceforth' (از + این + پس). تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you' used emphatically. ایمن مشو is a negative imperative: ایمن 'safe' + مشو (negative imperative of شدن) = 'do not become safe' i.e. 'do not feel secure.' از بدی is an ablative/ablative-of-cause phrase 'from/against harm.'
segments.s664-g238: که introduces the reason/explanatory clause. پاداش is 'reward/recompense' and ایزدی (post‑nominal adjective) qualifies it as 'divine.' پیش آیدت is a verbal phrase: پیش (adverb/particle) + آید (3rd sing. present/subjunctive of آمدن) with enclitic -ت 'to you' = 'may come upon you' or 'befall you.' The order places the noun before its adjective and the verb after the particle + clitic, typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s664-g239: تو پدرود باش = 'be thou paderud' (literally 'be farewell' or idiomatically 'may you be accursed/damned'); تو is emphatic 'you,' پدرود functions as a predicative/exclamatory curse or farewell-like formula, and باش (imperative of بودن) issues the command/wish 'be.' The following ای بداندیش مرد is the vocative 'O ill‑minded man,' placed after the predicate for poetic emphasis.
segments.s664-g240: بد آید is a jussive/optative phrasing 'may/shall ill/evil come' (بد = 'bad/evil' + آید 'come/happen'). به رویت = 'to/upon your face' (idiomatically 'upon you' or 'before you') and ز بد کارکرد is an ablative phrase 'from/because of bad deeds' (ز = 'from/because of', بد modifies کارکرد 'deeds/performance'), giving the cause of the ill that will befall him.
segments.s664-g49: The prepositional phrase 'به خشنودی کردگار جهان' literally puts the purpose ('for the pleasure/approval') before the verb; 'به' marks purpose/benefit, 'خشنودی' is the abstract noun 'pleasure/approval', and 'کردگار جهان' is a genitive compound 'Creator of the world' (کردگار governed by جهان). This whole phrase answers 'for whom/what should one strive?'
segments.s664-g50: 'بکوشید' is an exhortative verb form meaning 'strive/endeavor' (here a jussive/imperative plural or general injunction). It is followed by 'یک‌سر' ('entirely/completely') as an adverb modifying the verb. 'کهان و مهان' is a coordinated nominal pair appended after the adverb: syntactically it is the scope or audience of that striving (i.e. 'both high and low / great and humble'); the conjunction 'و' simply links the two nouns.
segments.s664-g51: 'دگر آنک' is a classical demonstrative/contrastive start meaning 'as for the other/that one' (here introducing a contrast). 'مغزش بود پرخرد' is a copular clause: 'مغزش' (his brain/mind) is the subject with the possessive clitic '-ش', 'بود' is the past copula 'was', and 'پرخرد' is a predicate adjective 'full of wisdom/very wise'.
segments.s664-g52: 'سوی ناسپاسی دلش ننگرد' is a negative present/state clause where 'سوی ناسپاسی' ('toward ingratitude') is a prepositional phrase (سوی + noun), 'دلش' is the topic/subject 'his heart' with clitic '-ش', and 'ننگرد' is a negative-present/subjunctive-form of the verb 'نگر' meaning 'turn/look/regard' (here 'does not turn/regard'). Word order places the directional prepositional phrase first for emphasis.
segments.s664-g53: This adverbial temporal clause begins with 'چو' = 'when/if'. 'نیکی فزایی' is a verbal-nominal construction: 'نیکی' (goodness/deed) and 'فزایی' (causal/imperative or second-person verb-stem 'you increase/multiply'); together they mean 'when you increase kindness'. 'بروی کسان' places the directional/beneficiary phrase after the verb: 'toward/for people' (بـ- + روی here expresses direction or application to people).
segments.s664-g54: This main clause: 'بود مزد آن سوی تو نارسان' has 'بود' as the past/copular auxiliary 'was/is', 'مزد' the noun 'reward', and 'آن سوی' a locative phrase 'on the other side' (idiomatically the next world). 'تو' is the vocative/second-person address and 'نارسان' is an adjectival/epithetical word appended to qualify or contrast the worldly scene. The line therefore contrasts the locus of reward (the other side) with the burning/transient character of this world.
segments.s664-g55: 'میامیز با مردم کژ گوی' is an instructive clause where 'میامیز' is the present-form verb meaning 'mix/associate' (in this context given as an admonition—more naturally rendered 'do not associate' depending on the surrounding negative sense), 'با مردم' is the prepositional object 'with people', and 'کژ گوی' is a postpositive adjective phrase 'crooked-speaking' (a compound of adjective 'کژ' and participle/agentive 'گوی') describing those people.
segments.s664-g56: The subordinate clause 'که او را نباشد سخن جز بروی' begins with 'که' introducing reason/explanation ('for/because'). 'او را' marks the person as object/topic (the clitic 'را' giving emphasis), 'نباشد' is a negative existential/verb form meaning 'there is not / he does not have', 'سخن' is 'speech/words', and 'جز بروی' ('except / except to go') uses 'جز' ('except') followed by 'بروی' (here an infinitival/verb form meaning 'to go/leave'). Together the clause says that such a person has no speech except words of departure/parting; note the classical order places the exception at the end for emphasis.
segments.s664-g9: نخست is an adverb 'first/initially' placed before the verb phrase for emphasis. آفرین کرد is a compound where آفرین (a noun/nominalized exclamation 'praise') combines with the past tense auxiliary/verb کرد ('did/made') to mean 'praised' or 'offered praise to'. بر is the preposition 'to/unto' linking the action to the following object کردگار.
segments.s664-g10: This is a noun phrase in apposition to the object of praise. توانا ('powerful/mighty') and داننده ('knower') are coordinated by و. روزگار is the noun modified by داننده: 'knower of روزگار' (here 'fate/destiny'), so the whole phrase describes the Creator praised above.
segments.s664-g11: دگر گفت here functions like 'then/again he said' (a sequential marker plus verb). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (the supplied senses did not include the verbal sense, so no exact sense index is available). ما is the first-person plural subject 'we'. تخت نامی کنیم uses تخت 'throne' + نامی 'famous/renowned' and the verb کنیم in the present/jussive subjunctive form meaning 'let us make' or 'we shall make'—a hortative/optative construction.
segments.s664-g12: گرانمایگان را is the definite/plural object 'the illustrious ones' (را marks the definite object). گرامی is an adjective 'honored/respected' and کنیم repeats the hortative/subjunctive 'let us make/honor'—the repetition of کنیم makes two coordinated imperatives/hortatives in one sentence.
segments.s664-g13: جهان را is the definite object 'the world' (را marks it). بداریم is the present/subjunctive form of داشتن used here in the sense 'let us keep/hold' (hortative). در زیر پر is a prepositional phrase 'under (the) wing' where در + زیر intensifies location 'in/under beneath the wing'—پر is 'feather/wing' used metaphorically.
segments.s664-g14: چنان چون is a comparative 'just as/like' (چنان … چون = 'such as' / 'as when'). پدر داشت is a past clause 'the father had/held'—داشت is past 'had' describing how the father behaved. با داد و فر lists two qualities 'with justice (داد) and glory (فر)', joined by و.
segments.s664-g15: گنه‌کردگان را is the definite object 'the sinners' (را marks the object). هراسان is an adjective/participle 'frightened' used here predicatively with کنیم: هراسان کنیم = 'let us make (them) frightened' = 'let us terrify'. کنیم is the hortative/subjunctive 'let us do'.
segments.s664-g16: ستم‌دیدگان را 'the oppressed/wronged' (object marker را). تن‌آسان is a compound meaning 'at ease/comforted' or 'having bodily ease', and کنیم again gives the hortative sense 'let us make/let us bring (them) comfort'—parallel structure to the previous clause.
segments.s664-g265: Subject 'تو' (you) comes first in the literary word order. The prepositional phrase 'با نامداران ایران' ('with the renowned/illustrious men of Iran') follows the subject and precedes the verb. The verb form 'بیای' is an older/subjunctive/imperative-type form meaning 'come' (attaching to 'تو'); in this poetic context it issues the command/insistence that you should come along.
segments.s664-g266: 'همی‌باش' is an imperative/hortative present form meaning 'be (present/remain)' — here a command to stay. 'در پیش تختم' is a locative phrase, 'در پیش' = 'before/in the presence of' and 'تختم' = 'my throne' (تخت + possessive -م). The closing verb 'بپای' (from پا/پای with the prefix بـ) means 'abide/stand by' or 'remain'; the two verbs form a pair of imperatives advising the addressee where and how to be.
segments.s664-g267: The preposition 'ز' (poetic for از) means 'from/of' and introduces the source of the question: 'سیمای برزینت' = 'the face/visage of Barzin' (برزین + the possessive suffix -ت 'your' is attached inside برزینت). The verb 'پرسم' is first‑person narrative (here 'I will ask' / 'I ask') and 'سخن' ('words/question') is the object: literally 'From Barzin's face I will ask speech' → 'I will address a question to Barzin.'
segments.s664-g268: 'چو' is temporal/subordinating and here means 'when'. 'پاسخ گزاری' is a verb phrase 'you put/give an answer' (گزاری from گزار/گزارد used with the object 'پاسخ'). 'دلت' is 'your heart' (دل + 2nd‑person suffix -ت). 'نرم کن' is the imperative 'make (it) soft' — a two‑word imperative where نرم is adjective 'soft' and کن is the imperative 'do/make'.
segments.s664-g269: 'بپرسم' is the narrator's verb 'I will ask' (classical narrative/subjunctive form). The complement clause introduced by 'که' contains 'این دوستار تو' ('this admirer/devotee of yours' — این + دوستار + possessive تو) and the question word 'کی' ('who'), followed by the copula 'ست' ('is'). The whole segment means 'I will ask who this admirer of yours is.'
segments.s664-g270: This is a following subordinate/conditional clause. 'بدست' (به‌دست) functions as a prepositional/agentive marker 'by/through' (poetic 'بدست' = 'by means of'); 'ار' is the archaic conditional 'if' (introducing an alternative possibility); then 'پرستنده ایزدیست' = 'he is a worshipper/devotee of the divine' (پرستنده = worshipper/devotee, ایزدیست = 'is of the divine/of God' with the copula fused). Together: '...whether/if he is a worshipper of the divine.'
segments.s664-g271: 'تو' is the addressed person. 'پاسخ چنین ده' is an imperative instruction: 'give such an answer' (ده = imperative of 'to give'). The following 'که' introduces the content of that report clause: 'این بدتنست' = 'that this one is ill-bodied' (بدتنست = بدتن + ست, a predicate 'is ill-bodied').
segments.s664-g272: 'بداندیش' is an adjectival predicate 'malicious / ill‑minded'. The next token 'وز' here functions as the fused contraction 'و ز' = 'and from' (classical poetic joining of و + ز), introducing origin; this sense is not in the provided list. 'تخم' = 'seed/origin' and 'آهرمنست' is a predicate form meaning 'is of Ahriman' (آهرمن + -ست). The clause therefore says 'and (he) is ill‑minded, and of the seed (i.e., offspring) of Ahriman.'
segments.s664-g369: نهاده is a past participle/perfect meaning 'placed' and appears before its locative phrases (typical of the older, poetic word order). به صندوق is a prepositional phrase 'into/into the chest' (به here = 'into/towards'), در introduces a nested locative 'in' and حقه‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a small pouch' (حقه + -ای). Together they specify where the item was placed.
segments.s664-g370: بحقه is here functioning as a fused locative-like phrase (بِ حقّه = 'in/at the pouch'); the provided lexical senses do not include this locative use, so it is marked unknown. درون 'inside' reinforces the location; پارسی qualifies رقعه‌ای as 'a note in Persian' (پارسی = the Persian language) and رقعه‌ای is an indefinite 'note'. Word order places the description of the inner contents after the locative marker.
segments.s664-g371: نبشتست is an archaic/perfect passive-like form meaning 'it is written' (a fixed literary formula). بر is the preposition 'on', and پرنیان سپید is a noun + adjective sequence 'white silk' (پرنیان = silken cloth; سپید = white). The inscription's medium is therefore specified as 'written on white silk.'
segments.s664-g372: بدان is an old adverbial phrase meaning 'by that' or 'thereby' (here presented in two pieces in the annotation); it links the written statement to its effect. باشد is the 3rd-person jussive/subjunctive 'may be / shall be' (a literary optative used to state what will hold). ایرانیان را uses را as the object/topic marker 'to the Iranians', and امید is the predicate 'hope' — literal order: 'by that shall be to the Iranians hope.'
segments.s664-g373: به خط پدرت is a genitive locative 'in the hand/handwriting of your father' where به + خط = 'in/by the hand (script) of' and پدرت is 'your father' (پدر + 2nd-person suffix ت). آن جهاندار شاه is an appositive nominal phrase 'that world-ruling king' identifying the father; note the demonstrative آن preceding the titles, typical of epic diction.
segments.s664-g374: This clause reports the content of the writing as an order: تو را (تو + object marker را) marks 'you' as the one affected. اندر‌ان here means 'therein'/'in it' (i.e. in that document/place). کرد is the main past verb 'made/did/ordered' and is followed by باید نگاه, 'you must keep watch' where باید expresses necessity and نگاه (literally 'look/gaze') functions as 'guard/watch' in this context. The older word order places the object and locative adjacent to the verb and the deontic phrase after it.
segments.s664-g375: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; هرمز is a proper name here (a person) and شنید is the past verb 'heard/learned'. آن refers back to the preceding news, and فرستاد کس is 'sent someone' (فرستاد = sent; کس = someone). The clause is a straightforward temporal-action construction.
segments.s664-g376: به نزدیک is a directional phrase 'to/near' (به = to/toward, نزدیک = near), گنجور here denotes the treasure-house/treasury (a place) and فریادرس is the agentive noun 'rescuer' or 'one who brings succor'. Together they describe whom/where he sent someone: 'to the nearby treasury, a rescuer.'
segments.s664-g17: This is a poetic inversion: the predicate phrase ستون بزرگیست ('is a great pillar') precedes the noun آهستگی, which here is the subject. بزرگیست contains the copula -ست in a fused form (بزرگ + یست), so the whole means 'is a great pillar' with آهستگی as the thing being predicated; آهستگی is a nominal subject meaning gentleness/forbearance.
segments.s664-g18: همان is a demonstrative linking back to the previous clause ('that/that very [pillar]'); the clause is nominal, listing coordinated qualities (بخشش و داد و شایستگی) with no verb — a common poetic ellipsis equating 'that [pillar] is generosity, justice, and worthiness.' داد here means 'justice' rather than the verb 'gave'.
segments.s664-g19: بدانید is an imperative (2nd-person plural) 'know/be aware'; کز is the classical contraction of که + ز and here introduces the subordinate clause ('that / that from'), linking to the following phrase کردگار جهان as the agent/topic of what will be said — 'the Creator of the world'.
segments.s664-g20: A compact nominal clause: the coordinated nouns بد و نیک ('bad and good') are the semantic subject, followed by the negative temporal adverb هرگز 'never' and the verb نماند 'remain' (3rd singular, present/persistent sense). نهان is the predicate/adjective 'hidden'. Together: 'bad and good never remain hidden.'
segments.s664-g21: A noun phrase in apposition: نیاگان ما 'our ancestors' is followed by a descriptive title تاج‌داران دهر ('crown-bearers of the age'), where تاج‌داران is a plural agent noun and دهر is a genitive modifier 'of the age/world'. The whole names the referent that the next relative clause will modify.
segments.s664-g22: Relative clause introduced by که ('who/that') modifying the ancestors. از here has a causal/ablative force 'because of / on account of', دادشان 'their justice' is the reason, آفرین functions as a nominal predicate 'praise/acclaim', بود is the copula 'was', and بهر means 'for' or 'on behalf of' — together meaning roughly 'who, on account of their justice, were worthy of praise.'
segments.s664-g23: نجستند is a past-tense, 3rd-person-plural verb 'they sought' (here meaning 'they desired/searched for'); it takes جز ('except/only') to introduce the limited object: 'they sought nothing except ...' The coordinated nouns following are the things they sought (داد و بایستگی = 'justice and duty/what ought to be').
segments.s664-g24: A simple coordination of abstract nouns (بزرگی 'greatness', گردی, شایستگی 'worthiness'). The phrase continues the list of virtues they sought; note that گردی here is a poetic noun whose exact nuance depends on context (see senses field).
segments.s664-g393: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial phrase ('from that time on'); پس here means 'after/then'. پرآشوب is a compound adjective meaning 'full of turmoil'. گردد (from گشت/گرد) is a present/poetic copular/auxiliary meaning 'becomes'. The phrase order is temporal phrase → predicate → subject (typical poetic inversion), reading literally 'From then on becomes tumultuous the world.'
segments.s664-g394: شود is another present/poetic copular 'becomes/is made'. نام و آواز is a fixed pairing meaning 'name and reputation/fame' (آواز used metaphorically as 'reputation'). او is the third-person pronoun 'his'. در نهان is an adverbial phrase 'in secret' or 'hiddenly'. Word order places the verb before its nominal complement in a poetic inversion.
segments.s664-g395: پدید آید is a two-part poetic verb meaning 'appears' or 'comes into being' (پدید = 'appear', آید = 'comes'). از هر سویی is a prepositional phrase 'from every direction/quarter'. دشمنی is the abstract noun 'enmity/hostility' and functions as the thing that 'appears'. The verb precedes the source phrase and the noun, a common order in poetry.
segments.s664-g396: یکی introduces an item among others: 'one (of them)'. It apposes the previous 'دشمنی' to describe that enemy: بدنژادی 'of low birth/ignoble' and آهرمنی 'demonic/malignant' are two adjectival descriptors joined by و. There is no new verb; this is an appositive noun phrase describing the arisen enemy.
segments.s664-g397: پراگنده گردد is a verbal phrase 'become scattered / are spread'; گرد once again is the copular/auxiliary 'become'. ز هر سو is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from every side/quarter'. سپاه (army/host) appears after the verb as the syntactic subject in poetic inversion: 'armies become scattered from every side.'
segments.s664-g398: فروافگند is a past/simple verbal form meaning 'cast down/overthrew' used here in the narrative-poetic present-past register. The direct object is دشمن او را ('his enemy' with را marking definiteness/object). ز گاه is an ablative/time phrase 'from (or at) time' used idiomatically as 'from time to time' or 'at occasions.' The clause says that (the hosts) overthrow his enemy at times.
segments.s664-g399: دو چشمش ('his two eyes') is the definite object (چشم + possessive -ش). کند is the causative/performative verb 'make/do' (classical poetic present or simple narrative). کور is the adjective 'blind' functioning as object complement: 'make [him] blind.' The sequence خویش زنش is a compressed/poetic expression meaning 'his own wife' (خویش emphasizing 'own' and زنِش = 'his wife'), placed after the clause in apposition or as the agent in a somewhat inverted order — i.e. 'his own wife made his eyes blind.'
segments.s664-g400: ازان پس is again a temporal marker 'from that time on / thereafter'. برآرند is a third-person plural verbal form ('they bring forth / they take away'); in context it functions causatively/performatively meaning 'they deprive' or 'they remove.' هوش از تنش literally 'the awareness/senses from his body' is the object phrase, so the clause means 'they take awareness from his body' i.e. they deprive him of consciousness or life. Note the plural verb agrees with implied agents (the attackers).
segments.s664-g353: یکی پند گویم = 'I will give one piece of advice.' یکی is the numeral/indefinite 'one/a certain' modifying پند 'advice.' گویم is the 1st-person present (subjunctive/indicative) of گفتن used here to mean 'I say / I will say / I give (advice).' چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; خوانی is 2nd-person singular present of خواندن in the sense 'to call/summon' (so 'when you summon'), and مرا is the object 'me' (here split into the clitic parts in the word list).
segments.s664-g354: بر تخت شاهی نشانی مرا = 'place/mark me on the royal throne.' بر تخت شاهی is a prepositional phrase 'upon the royal throne' (بر = on, تخت = throne, شاهی = royal/kingship as adjective). نشانی functions as 'a place/mark/seat' here (a noun meaning 'a place/marking to set someone'), and مرا is the object 'me' (again expressed by the clitic). The whole NP answers where the speaker wants to be placed.
segments.s664-g355: تو را سودمندیست از پند من = 'My advice will be beneficial to you.' تو را marks 'you' as the experiencer/beneficiary (تو + object marker را). سودمندیست is a predicative compound meaning 'is beneficial / will be of use' (stative verb 'بود' fused into the form); از introduces the source 'from/of' and پند من = 'my counsel' (پند 'advice' + من as possessive 'my').
segments.s664-g356: به زندان بمان یک زمان بند من = 'Stay in prison for a while, my captive.' به زندان بمان is an imperative/imperative-like injunction: به + زندان (into/at prison) + بمان (2nd-person imperative/optative of ماندن 'to remain'). یک زمان = 'for a time / awhile' (a temporal adverbial). بند من is a vocative/possessive phrase 'my captive' or 'my fettered one' (بند used of someone bound or a captive, with من marking 'my').
segments.s664-g357: به ایران تو را سودمندی بود = 'You would be useful to Iran.' به ایران is a dative/benefactive phrase 'to/for Iran.' تو را again marks 'you' as beneficiary/topic. سودمندی (usefulness) is the predicate and بود is the past/remote copula (here rendering a potential/conditional sense: 'would be / would have been'). The overall structure is 'To Iran you would be usefulness.'
segments.s664-g358: خردمند را بی‌گزندی بود = 'The wise would be unharmed.' خردمند را marks 'the wise (man)' as topic/subject. بی‌گزندی is a nominal/adjectival compound meaning 'freedom from harm / unharmed,' and بود is again the copula giving past/conditional sense ('would be / would remain'). The clause states a general result: the wise one would be safe.
segments.s664-g359: پیامش چو نزدیک هرمز رسید = 'When his message drew near Hormoz / reached near Hormoz.' پیامش is پیام 'message' + the 3rd-person clitic ش 'his' (indicating 'his message'). چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; نزدیک is an adverb/adjective 'near/approaching'; هرمز is a proper name; رسید is the past tense 'arrived / reached.' The clause is a temporal subordinate ('when his message reached...').
segments.s664-g360: یکی رازدار از میان برگزید = 'He chose a certain confidant from among (them/others).' یکی is 'one / a certain (one)' used to introduce an indefinite person; رازدار is the noun 'confidant / keeper of secrets;' از میان is the idiom 'from among'; برگزید is the simple past 'he chose / selected.' The object is indefinite and chosen from a group.
segments.s664-g273: This is a temporal + imperative clause. وزان پس ('from that time / thereafter') sets the time; ز من is the prepositional object 'from me / of me'; هرچ خواهی is a relative phrase 'whatever you want' where خواهی is 2nd-person present 'you want'; بخواه is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'ask' (i.e., 'ask [for it]'). Word order is classical: temporal phrase → source → object/relative → imperative.
segments.s664-g274: A simple asyndetic list of objects (things to be asked for) joined by و 'and'. These nouns are direct objects of the preceding imperative بخواه ('ask for'). There is no article; items are given as general offers: a devotee/servant, a throne, love/affection, and a hat/crown.
segments.s664-g275: بدو 'to him' (dative) + گفت (past 'said') with subject name بهرام following in the classical inverted order: 'بدو گفت بهرام' = 'Bahram said to him.' کایدون کنم is an object/clause meaning 'shall I make (him) a schemer' where کنم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive used to express intention or volition.
segments.s664-g276: ازین بد 'from/ because of this evil' (از + این regularized as ین) introduces the cause; که گفتی is a relative clause 'that you said' (گفتی = 'you said'); صدافزون کنم is the verb phrase 'I will make [it] a hundredfold' with کنم expressing the speaker's resolve (optative/volitional use of the present-subjunctive).
segments.s664-g277: بسیمای 'with the face/appearance of' + برزین (proper name) establishes a resemblance: 'in the appearance of Barzin.' The relative clause که بود از مهان 'who was from the guests' uses بود (past 'was') to describe Barzin's status among مهان ('guests' / noble visitors).
segments.s664-g278: گزین here functions as an epithet 'chosen/selected' modifying پدرش 'his father' (possessive suffix -ش). آن چراغ جهان is an appositive epithet 'that lamp of the world' describing the father. Classical Persian often places descriptive epithets (گزین, آن چراغ جهان) adjacent to the noun they qualify.
segments.s664-g279: همی‌ساخت is an imperfect/progressive past 'was making/contriving.' تا introduces purpose/result ('so that / in order that') and چاره‌ای is 'a remedy/means'; چون کند is a free interrogative/subordinate 'how he might do [it]' where کند is subjunctive/optative 'may do' expressing potential action. Overall: 'he was contriving so that [he could find] some means, how he might [do it]'.
segments.s664-g280: که introduces the subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that / in order that'); پیراهن مهر is the direct object 'the coat/shirt of love' (مهر = 'love/affection'); بیرون کند is the verbal phrase 'to take out/remove' where کند is again subjunctive/optative indicating the desired or potential action ('may take off').
segments.s664-g161: This is a poetic enumerative phrase: ز (a shortened/poetic از) introduces items of possession or ornament; وز here is the archaic/elevated sequence 'و ز' linking each noun as 'and with'. The list — دینار, گنج, خواسته — are nouns in series describing what someone is furnished with; the verb (آراسته) appears later in the next segment and predicates this whole series.
segments.s664-g162: هم 'also' adds another category; از introduces the source/thing one is adorned with (here functioning like 'with/of'); کاخ و ایوان are coordinated nouns joined by و; آراسته is a past participle/adjectival predicate ('adorned') placed at the end, so the whole clause reads 'also adorned with palace and iwan'. The subject is omitted (understood from context).
segments.s664-g163: به موبد 'to the mōbed' places the indirect object first (object–verb order is common in classical Persian); چنین گفت 'thus said' is the reporting phrase in the past tense (گفت). کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing the addressee, and نامجوی is the proper name of the addressee. The verb 'گفت' here is past third-person and introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.s664-g164: چو 'when' introduces a temporal subordinate clause; رفتی is second-person past 'you went/when you go' used as a temporal marker; از ایدر 'from here' (ایدر = here) and به هرمزد 'to Ahura Mazda' give direction. گوی at the end is an old/formal present/imperfect imperative 'say' (tell him) — the imperative/command closes the subordinate clause and directs the addressee what to do on going.
segments.s664-g165: که‌گر is a conditional 'if' (classical compound of که + گر); سرنپیچی is a verbal-noun / action meaning 'turning away' (i.e., refusing or not heeding); ز گفتار من marks the source/object of turning away 'from my words/counsel'. The clause sets the condition for the admonition that follows.
segments.s664-g166: براندیشی is a second-person exhortation/imperative or jussive form meaning 'consider/reflect' (addressing the same 'you'); از رنج و تیمار من is the object 'on/over my suffering and care' with من functioning possessively. The verb comes before its object here, typical of Persian, and connects the conditional to the moral appeal.
segments.s664-g167: که here introduces an explanatory/cause clause ('for' / 'that'); از شهریاران تو uses از to mark the agent/source ('at the hands of your rulers/princes'); خورده‌ام is the perfect of خوردن used in the sense 'has befallen me / I have suffered' (a common idiom meaning 'I have been harmed/treated thus by'). The clause explains why the speaker issues the warning.
segments.s664-g168: تو را 'you' with the object marker را marks the direct object; نیز 'also' adds parallel emphasis; در بر is an idiomatic locution 'in the bosom/embrace' meaning 'under my protection'; بپرورده‌ام is perfect 'I have nurtured/raised' with the prefixed ب- and the 1st-person singular ending, so the clause asserts the speaker's past care for the addressee.
segments.s664-g25: Two parallel noun phrases introduced by the poetic preposition ز (here 'from/because of'), listing causes: 'ز کهتر پرستش' = 'from the worship of the lesser' and 'ز مهتر نواز' = 'from flattering/treating the greater kindly'. Word order is poetic (preposition + noun phrase). 'نواز' is used as a verbal-noun/adjective meaning 'treat kindly / flatter'.
segments.s664-g26: بداندیش را داشتن is a light nominal/verbal construction meaning 'to hold/harbour ill thoughts'; را is the definite/object/topic marker giving emphasis to the object (here the hostile thought). در گداز is a prepositional phrase ('in g͟dāz') where گداز is used metaphorically as 'burning/ardor/anguish'.
segments.s664-g27: به هر کشوری is a locative/limiting phrase 'in/for every country/realm'. دست و فرمان is a coordinated nominal subject meaning 'power and command'. مراست is a poetic contraction of 'مرا است' ('is mine / belongs to me'), so the whole clause means 'power and command are mine.'
segments.s664-g28: A second coordinated predicate list (توانایی و داد و پیمان) followed by the same predicate مراست (poetic 'مرا است'), i.e. 'ability, justice, and covenant are mine.' Each noun is enumerated and bound to the copular possession expressed by مراست.
segments.s664-g29: A relative clause introduced by کسی را که: 'the one whom...'؛ یزدان کند پادشا = 'God makes (him) king.' کند is the classical present/subjunctive form of 'to do/make' used here as 'makes.' را marks کسی as the definite/target of the relative clause.
segments.s664-g30: بنازد is an older/classical verbal form meaning 'let (someone) be honored/let homage be shown' (an optative/impersonal exhortation); بدو = 'to him'; مردم پارسا = 'the pious people' (the agents who should honor him). The verb is directed toward the dative phrase بدو.
segments.s664-g31: که begins a clause expressing a general truth: 'that / namely'; سرمایه شاه = 'the king's capital/source/asset' (i.e. the king's true resource); بخشایشست is 'بخشایش + است' (copula attached) here meaning 'is generosity/mercy' — the clause asserts that a ruler's true wealth is generosity.
segments.s664-g32: زمانه is used as 'the world / the times / public fortune'; ز بخشش = 'because of/by generosity' (ز used causally/poetically); به آسایشست = 'is in comfort/at peace' (به + آسایش + est). The clause states that the world enjoys peace through generosity.
segments.s664-g217: بدو گفت موبد is an inverted word order: 'بدو' (to him) + گفت موبد (said the mobed) — literally 'to him said the mobed'.
