The conjunction که introduces a reported clause that follows ('that the king's command...'). فرمان شاه is a noun‑noun possessive (the king's command) with the possessor after the possessed as is normal in Persian.
segments.s664-g218: بیامد is the past form of آمدن used here as 'has come/arrived'. نماند is past of ماندن meaning 'remains/was left' (here in sense 'no longer remains' in the negative construction that follows). مرا را رای و راه: مرا is the first‑person object/benefactive ('for me' / 'to me'), را is the object/definite marker attached in this older word‑order, and رای و راه are the two coordinated nouns 'counsel/plan and way/method' as the things that do not remain.
segments.s664-g219: بخورد is a past tense (he ate). و ز خوان is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the table/banquet'; ز is the poetic form of از. زار و پیچان are descriptive modifiers of the meal/scene—زار ('in sorrow' / lamenting) and پیچان ('winding, twisted' or figuratively 'tortuous')—and برفت is the past of رفتن 'he departed/left' (here 'he went away from the table').
segments.s664-g220: همی‌راند is an imperfect/progressive form with the archaic prefix همی‑ meaning 'he was riding/driving on' or 'he rode on'. تا here means 'as far as / until' and introduces the goal clause. خانه خویش is a possessive phrase 'his own house' split here into خوی + ش (older orthography), and تفت is a place name (Taft) — so the whole phrase means 'as far as his house, Taft.'
segments.s664-g221: ازان خوردن زهر is a genitive/deverbal noun phrase: ازان ('from that') + خوردن (the act of eating) + زهر (poison) — 'about that eating of poison'. با کس نگفت is a negative past construction 'he did not tell anyone' (کس with the negative verb carries the meaning 'no one').
segments.s664-g222: یکی here is an indefinite subject 'one / a certain (he)' used to mean 'he then' or 'one of them'; جامه افگند is a simple past clause 'threw off a garment' (افگند = threw/cast). نالان is an adverbial or participial descriptor 'lamenting/mourning', and بخفت is past tense 'he slept' (fell asleep).
segments.s664-g223: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb used for royal speech meaning 'he commanded/ordered/said'. تا here introduces the subordinate clause of purpose/command ('so that / that they should...'). پادزهر is the compound noun 'antidote', and آورند is the jussive/subjunctive third‑person plural 'they bring' used after تا to express the commanded action.
segments.s664-g224: ازان introduces the source 'from those (treasures)'. گنج‌ها (treasures) appears here with a conditional/contrastive گر (if/and/or) giving an alternative source; the line presents alternative sources for the antidote ('from those treasuries — or, if not, from the city'). ز شهر آورند repeats the verb (آورند) with z + شهر 'from the city' as the alternative source.
segments.s664-g377: که introduces a relative/nominal clause ('that' / 'who'); در گنجهای پدر is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the father's treasuries' (در + plural گنجهای + possessor پدر). بازجوی is an agentive/participial noun 'the one who searched' or 'searcher' and functions as the implied subject/actor of the clause (archaic word order: locative phrase before the agent noun).
segments.s664-g378: یکی (numeral) + ساده (adjective) + صندوق (noun) = 'one/plain chest'; و links the next phrase. مهری بروی is a compact/archaic phrase meaning 'a seal upon it' (بروی = 'on/upon' in older spelling/usage); overall this segment gives the object found. Word order places the numeral and adjective before the noun, and the locative/relational particle 'بروی' attaches to indicate location/position of the مهر.
segments.s664-g379: بران is an adverbial/locution 'thereupon' or 'on that'; مهر is the noun 'seal' and بر (here) is the preposition 'on' governing نام 'name'; نوشین‌روان is the proper epithet/name attached to نام. The phrase literally reads 'thereupon a seal on the name Nushin‑ravan' = 'a seal bearing the name Nushin‑ravan.'
segments.s664-g380: که here introduces an optative/blessing clause 'that/may'. جاوید 'eternal' followed by the optative particle بادا ('may it be') forms the formula 'may it be eternal'; روانش is 'his soul' (روان + possessive clitic -ش) and جوان is the predicate adjective 'young'. Word order places the optative formula before the nominal predicate: 'that may his soul be eternal and young.'
segments.s664-g381: هم‌اکنون is an adverb 'right now'; شب تیره is the noun phrase 'the dark night' (noun + adjectival تیره). پیش من is a prepositional phrase 'before me / in my presence'. آر is an archaic/poetic imperative meaning 'bring' (here used as a command). The overall clause is an immediate imperative: 'Bring the dark night before me at once.'
segments.s664-g382: فراوان adverbially modifies the verbal idea 'بجستن' (to seek/search), placed before the infinitive; مبر is a negative imperative 'do not (take/do)' that negates the following action; روزگار is the object 'the times / fortunes'. The sequence means 'do not search the fortunes at length' or 'do not spend much time seeking about fate.' Note the negative imperative mبر + infinitive construction is characteristic of older/formal Persian.
segments.s664-g383: شتابید is a past-tense/remote perfect verb 'he hurried'. گنجور here denotes 'the treasury/treasure-house' (place approached) and is placed after the verb (verb–object order is common in older narrative Persian). و links the next action; صندوق جست uses جست (here 'sought/searched') with صندوق as its object: 'and (he) searched the chest.' The subject is understood from context and is shared across the coordinated verbs.
segments.s664-g384: بیاورد is a past-tense narrative verb 'he brought' (3rd person). پویان functions as the agent/subject here (likely a proper name 'Poyan' or 'the seeker' used as a name) placed after the verb, a typical poetic order. به مهر درست is a prepositional phrase 'with the correct seal' where به = 'with/on', مهر = 'seal', and درست is a postpositive adjective 'proper/right'. The whole clause: 'Poyan brought it with the proper seal.'
segments.s664-g89: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید here functions as an impersonal/passive 'was heard' (classical usage) governing the following phrase گفتار او 'his speech'. The verb precedes the subject انجمن in a poetic inversion: 'When his speech was heard, the assembly...'.
segments.s664-g90: پر اندیشه گشتند is a predicate meaning 'became full of thought', with گشتند (pl. past) agreeing with the people of the assembly. زان is causal 'from/thereupon', and تن‌به‌تن is an adverbial phrase 'one‑on‑one' describing how they spoke or separated.
segments.s664-g91: سر گنج‌داران is a possessive phrase 'the head/mind of the treasurers' (ezāfe omitted in poetry). پر از بیم گشت is an intransitive past clause 'became full of fear' (گشت as 'became'), describing their state.
segments.s664-g92: ستمکاره را topicalizes or marks the affected person ('the oppressor'). دل به دو نیم گشت literally 'the heart became into two halves' = 'his heart split in two', with به used directionally 'into' and گشت 'became'.
segments.s664-g93: خردمند و درویش lists two kinds of people 'wise and dervish'; زان 'because of that' links back to the preceding events, and هرک بود 'whoever was' is a relative/generalizing phrase (هرک = 'whoever'; بود = past 'was').
segments.s664-g94: به دلش اندرون places the location 'within his heart' (دل + enclitic ش = 'his'), with به ... اندرون functioning together as 'into/within'. شادمانی فزود is a short past clause 'joy increased' (فزود = 'increased').
segments.s664-g95: چنین بود تا شد is 'thus it was until it became...' with تا as 'until'. بزرگیش راست pairs the noun 'his greatness' with راست ('true/straight/established'), meaning his greatness came to be firm/established.
segments.s664-g96: هر آن چیز is a noun phrase 'every/any such thing', در پادشاهی 'in the kingdom' is the locus, and که خواست is a relative clause 'that he wanted/desired' modifying 'thing'. The clause as a whole means 'everything in the kingdom that he desired.'
segments.s664-g177: ز here is the causal/prepositional z- used as 'because of' before a proper name (classical/poetic usage). ایزد‌گشسب is the name (the cause). آنگهی + شد forms a past-time sequence: آنگهی 'then/thereupon' + شد 'became' (شد as main past verb). درشت is an adjectival predicate meaning 'harsh/rough/angry' describing the subject (omitted here, understood from context).
segments.s664-g178: به زندان فرستاد is a straightforward past action: به marks motion 'into/toward', زندان is the destination object, and فرستاد is the past tense 'sent'. و links the second clause. او را is the object with the definite/object marker را placed after the pronoun, and بکشت is the past verb 'killed' (archaic/prefixation visible in narrative style). Word order is typical older Persian: (he) sent [him] to prison and (he) killed him.
segments.s664-g179: سخنهای موبد is a genitive/group: سخنهای (plural 'words/talks') qualified by موبد ('the mobed's'). فراوان here intensifies/quantifies the words ('many'/'frequent'), and شنید is the simple past 'he heard'. The noun phrase precedes the verb as is normal in older Persian narrative.
segments.s664-g180: بر او بر نکرد is a negated verbal construction: بر کردن (literally 'to do/bring upon') with نکرد negating it, so 'did not do/bring upon him'. او is the indirect/affected person. ایچ گونه پدید is a nominal/adverbial phrase 'any such kind' or 'any such thing' (ایچ 'no/none' + گونه 'manner/kind'), with پدید 'to appear/happen' used idiomatically to mean 'such a thing come to pass' — here negated by نکرد.
segments.s664-g181: همی‌راند is a habitual/continuous past form ('was driving/kept driving') with the verb راندن; here it personifies mental motion. اندیشه is the subject/thing being driven ('thought'/'intention'). بر marks the domain of consideration 'upon' and is followed by the contrasted pair خوب و زشت ('good and bad'). The clause means his thoughts continually ran over good and bad.
segments.s664-g182: سوی is a preposition 'toward(s)' and introduces the direction/aim of thought. چاره is 'remedy/expedient' (the proposed solution). کشتن is the infinitive 'to kill' functioning as a noun phrase modifying چاره (i.e. 'the expedient of killing'), and زردهشت names the target (Zoroaster). Together they mean the thought was leaning toward the solution of killing Zoroaster.
segments.s664-g183: بفرمود is the honorific/simple past 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a purpose/subordinate clause here ('so that' / 'in order that'). زهر is the noun 'poison' and خوالیگرش is a following name/epithet specifying which poison (an obscure/compound term treated as a single modifier or name). The clause as a whole sets up the object of the command.
segments.s664-g184: نهانی is an adverb 'secretly'. برد here functions as the past verb 'he/they took/brought' (a classical narrative past; if the listed sense entries don't match, this is the conventional reading). پیش means 'before/in the presence of' (i.e. brought forward to someone). در یک خورش is an instrumental/place phrase 'in one dish' (در + numeral + خورش 'stew/dish'), specifying how it was presented.
segments.s664-g209: بدو (bedo) is the dative/clitic-like prepositional adverb 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (classical Persian word order allows the object/clitic before the verb and the subject after it); موبد is the subject 'the mobed/priest' following the verb for poetic word order. به جان و سرت is an oath formula: به + nouns ('by' or 'upon') introduces what one swears by, جان = 'life' and سرت = 'your head' (سر + enclitic -ت 'your').
segments.s664-g210: که introduces the reported/wished clause; جاوید is the adjective 'eternal' placed before بادا, where بادا is the optative particle 'may it be' (classical poetic wish). سر و افسرت are two coordinate nouns 'head and helmet' (و joins them); افسرت contains the 2nd-person enclitic -ت ('your helmet'), so the wish targets the addressee's head and helmet.
segments.s664-g211: کزین is the poetic contraction of 'از این' = 'from/of this' and introduces the noun phrase نوشه خوردن 'drinking of the draught' (نوشه = draught, خوردن here is the infinitive/gerund 'to drink/consumption'). نفرماییم is the 1st-person plural subjunctive/optative with the prohibitory negative prefix ن-: ن + فرماییم (here فرماییم = to order/permit) so the clause means 'we should not permit/order (this drinking)'.
segments.s664-g212: به سیری 'to/into satiety' uses به marking attainment; رسیدم is 1st-person singular past/perfect 'I reached/attained' (here 'I have become/sufficiently full'); نیفزاییم is the hortative/subjunctive 1st-person-plural of فزودن (with negative prefix نی- here used as 'let us not increase' or 'we should not add'). The two clauses together state refusal plus that the speaker is already satisfied.
segments.s664-g213: بدو here again is the dative adverb 'to him/unto them' placed before گفت; گفت is the verb 'said' (past). هرمز is the speaker's name (Ahura Mazda / Hormoz). به خورشید و ماه is the indirect object phrase 'to the Sun and the Moon' (به marks the recipients). The order (clitic/dative before verb, subject after) is typical of the epic/poetic style.
segments.s664-g214: به here introduces purpose/concern 'for' or 'concerning'; پاکی روان is a noun phrase 'the purity (پاکی) of the soul/spirit (روان)'. جهاندار شاه is a title-like apposition: جهاندار 'world-ruler/sovereign' qualifies شاه 'king' (epithet before noun in poetry). The whole phrase is what Hormoz addresses the Sun and Moon about.
segments.s664-g215: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that ...'; بستانی is 2nd-person singular optative/subjunctive or imperative-like form of ستاندن 'to take' (addressing someone: 'take'); این نوشه 'this draught' is the direct object; ز انگشت من = 'from my finger' (ز = 'from' + انگشت 'finger' with possessive من 'my').
segments.s664-g216: برین is the fused poetic form of بر این ('upon/over this'); آرزو = 'wish/desire'. نشکنی is negative 2nd-person singular subjunctive from شکستن 'to break' (ن- prohibitive + شکل subjunctive), so 'do not break'; پشت من = 'my back' (literally) — together an idiom meaning 'do not break my back over this wish' (i.e., do not cause me hardship/suffering for this desire).
segments.s664-g313: چه is an interrogative 'what'; دیدی is the past 2nd-person singular of دیدن ('did you see'). ز is the poetic preposition 'of/from' and من is the object 'me'. تا introduces the subordinate clause 'that' (classical use = 'that/so that'); تو یار منی is a nominal clause with the enclitic copula ی marking 'you are (my) friend' (تو = subject, یار = 'friend/companion', من = 'my/me').
segments.s664-g314: ز here means 'in/with respect to' (poetic), introducing the domain in which the accusation holds. کردار و گفتار is a coordinate noun phrase 'deed(s) and speech' (parallel concrete nouns). آهرمنی is an epithet meaning 'Ahrimanic' or 'demonic' modifying the coordinated phrase.
segments.s664-g315: بدو is the clitic + prepositional phrase 'to him' (bedo = 'to him/unto him'). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here the lexical entry list given is nominal, so the proper verbal sense is not among the supplied senses). بهرام آذرمهان is the speaker's name/title (Bahrām Āzar‑mehān).
segments.s664-g316: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing reported speech. تخمی here denotes 'a seed' (classical usage), and پراگنده‌ای is the adjective + indefinite ending meaning 'scattered' or 'a scattered one' (the -ای marks indefiniteness/quality). در جهان is the locative 'in the world'.
segments.s664-g317: کزان is a compound pronominal/adverbial 'from that / from which'. بر + نخستین marks 'upon/the foremost' or 'from the foremost' (بر here a preposition 'toward/on' used with نخستین 'the first/foremost'). تو is the subject 'you' and خواهی is the present/future 2nd-person form of خواستن ('you will want/you will seek'); درود is the object 'greeting/blessing'. Word order places the temporal/locative phrase up front and the verb near the end, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s664-g318: از آتش 'from fire' (از = 'from, out of'; آتش = 'fire'). نیابی is the negative-present/optative form of یافتن with the negative prefix ن- meaning 'you will not find/obtain' (lexical sense 'find/obtain'). مگر here means 'except/only' and تیره دود is a nominal phrase 'dark smoke' (تیره = dark/bleak; دود = smoke), i.e. 'you will find nothing from the fire except black smoke.'
segments.s664-g319: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. کسری is the royal name/title 'Khosrow' (subject of the subordinate clause). The tokenization shows م and را separately but together here they form مرا 'me' (accusative); because the lemma/senses supplied do not match this split, that item is flagged; را is the definite object marker. و تو را repeats the object phrase 'and you' (تو = you; را = object marker). پیش + خواند: پیش 'in the presence of/before' and خواند here is the verb 'called/summoned' (one of the verbal senses listed).
segments.s664-g320: بر تخت شاهنشهی is the prepositional phrase 'upon the imperial throne' (بر = on; تخت = throne; شاهنشهی = 'imperial' or 'regal' as an adjective/attribute). برنشاند is a past transitive verb 'set/placed/seat(ed)' and takes the previously mentioned objects (me and you) — the subject (Khosrow) seated them on the throne.
segments.s664-g129: همی is an adverbial particle meaning 'constantly / still' that frames the ongoing nature of the desire. خوردنی is a noun 'something to eat' (direct object), آرزوی is 'a wish/longing' (governed by the verbal predicate that follows), and آیدم is the first-person present-form 'comes to me' (literally 'comes to me' → idiomatically 'I desire'). Word order puts the ongoing marker first, then the object and nominalized wish, and the verb at the end.
segments.s664-g130: شکم گرسنه is a noun phrase 'hungry stomach/belly' (شکم as the possessor/experiencer, گرسنه as its adjective). رنج is a noun 'suffering' and بفزایدم is a 1st-person present form with the old preverb ب- ('I increase / I add'); the verb comes last and governs the preceding noun phrase as its object (i.e. 'I increase the suffering of my hungry belly').
segments.s664-g131: یکی here is an indefinite 'a certain / one' (i.e. 'one morsel' or 'some'), خوردنی is 'food/morsel', پاک is the adjective 'pure/clean', پیشم is the prepositional/adverbial phrase 'before me / to me', and فرست is an imperative/short present 'send' (the subject 'you' is omitted). The object (یکی خوردنی پاک) appears before the verb, which is typical in Persian word order.
segments.s664-g132: دوایی is the direct noun 'a remedy / medicine' (object). بدین should be read as a prepositional phrase ('ب-' + demonstrative) meaning 'for this' or 'to this' (here marking the target of the remedy); درد is 'pain' specifying which pain; ریشم is a possessive form 'my‑ریشم' (the manuscript lexeme supplied does not match the context, see senses). فرست again is the imperative 'send'. Grammatically, the string is: object (دوایی) + prepositional/target phrase (بدین درد ریشم) + verb (فرست).
segments.s664-g133: دل موبد is a genitive construction 'the heart of the mobed (priest)'. از introduces the cause 'from / because of' and links to درد ('pain') which is specified by the genitive phrase پیغام اوی ('the pain of his message') — اوی here is the possessive 'his'. Word order places the possessor (موبد) after دل, and the causal prepositional phrase follows.
segments.s664-g134: غمی گشت is 'became a grief' (غمی subject/compliment, گشت 'became'). زان جای is an adverbial 'from that place / from that moment' (زان = 'from that'), و links the second phrase, and آرام اوی is 'his calm/peace' (آرام) with the possessive اوی 'his'. The verb گشت applies to the first clause; the second noun phrase is coordinated and understood in relation to that change (i.e. his calm departed/was gone).
segments.s664-g135: چنان داد پاسخ = 'he gave such an answer' (چنان 'such', داد 'gave', پاسخ 'answer'). که introduces the resulting or reported clause ('that / so that / namely'), and از کار بند is an adjectival/adverbial phrase where از کار means 'from / in respect of the work' and بند (here) should be read as 'fetter / hindrance' (i.e. 'be hindered in your work' — بند functions as the notion of being bound/hindered).
segments.s664-g136: منال is 'attainment / desired result' (the thing hoped for). ار is the conditional 'if', نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive 'does not come / should not come', and به جانت گزند is a prepositional phrase 'harm to your life' (به = 'to', جانت = 'your life', گزند = 'harm'). The clause reads as a conditional reassurance: if the attainment does not come, harm will not befall you.
segments.s664-g41: هرآن‌کس که ... introduces a relative/generalizing clause: 'whoever/anyone who'. The phrase هرآن‌کس که هست از شما نیک‌بخت literally orders the elements as 'whoever that is of you fortunate' — هست is the copula ('is'), and از شما marks scope 'among you'. نیک‌بخت is an adjective describing that person.
segments.s664-g42: همه شاد باشید is a 2nd-person plural optative/imperative use of باشید ('may you be / be (you all)') with همه 'all/everyone' as vocative/subject. زین before a noun phrase here functions poetically ('from/with/at this') and links the following compound تاج و تخت ('crown and throne') as the cause or object of the joy.
segments.s664-g43: میان بزرگان is a locative prepositional phrase 'among the great ones'. In poetic Persian the copula can be omitted or the predicate follow directly: درخشش (subject) + مراست (predicate noun meaning 'ceremony/rite'), so the line reads 'among the nobles, my radiance is a ceremony.'
segments.s664-g44: چو here means 'when'/'as'. بخشایش داد is a past action 'gave/granted generosity' (داد = gave). The second clause و بخشش مراست repeats the nominal predicate pattern: 'and the giving itself is a ceremony' — بخشش is the noun 'giving/donation' and مراست again 'is a ceremony/rite'.
segments.s664-g45: شما is the (plural/formal) subject 'you'. مهربانی is the direct object 'kindness'. بافزون together with کنيد forms an exhortation 'make more / increase' — بافزون is an adverbial/derivative meaning 'increasingly / more' and کنيد is the 2nd-person-plural imperative/subjunctive of كردن 'to make/do'.
segments.s664-g46: ز (poetic for 'از') marks source 'from/out of', followed by دل 'heart'. کینه و آز are coordinated objects 'hatred and greed'. بیرون کنید is the composite verb 'send out / expel' with کنید (2pl imperative) and بیرون 'out/away' giving the imperative sense 'cast out'.
segments.s664-g47: هرآن‌کس که introduces 'whoever'. پرهیز کرد is the verbalized noun + کرد construction meaning 'refrained/abstained' (پرهیز 'abstinence' + کرد 'did'). از دو کار is the prepositional phrase 'from two deeds/actions' specifying what is avoided.
segments.s664-g48: نبیند is the negative present/optative form 'will not see / shall not behold' (ن- prefix + بیند). The subject/experiencer follows: دو چشمش 'his two eyes'. بد روزگار is the object phrase 'bad time/fate' meaning 'misfortune' that the eyes will not witness.
segments.s664-g225: فروخورد is a past simple 3rd-person verb meaning 'he swallowed'.و is the coordinating particle 'and' linking two past actions. نامد is here used in a past/passive sense 'was called' (an older/poetic form) and is followed by the prepositional phrase به کار ('into/to the task'), so the whole phrase means 'and was called/assigned to the task'.
segments.s664-g226: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (or 'out of'). هرمز here names the deity Ahura Mazda. به introduces the indirect object 'to' and یزدان is a synonymic divine name 'God'; بنالید is a past 3rd-person verb 'he moaned/complained', and زار is an adverbial/qualifying word rendering the lament as pitiable or wailing. The clause therefore says he wailed piteously to God (in connection with Hormoz).
segments.s664-g227: Word order is inverted for emphasis: یکی استواری ('a certain stalwart/man') is the direct object (= 'a stalwart one') and فرستاد is the verb 'sent'; شاه (the agent) appears after the verb in this poetic/order ('the king sent'). The phrase as a whole means 'the king sent a certain steadfast man'.
segments.s664-g228: بدان here is the prepositional phrase 'be + an' (ب + آن) meaning 'to that one / to him / there'. تا introduces a purpose clause 'so that / in order that', and کند is the subjunctive/present-form 'may do' (third person). کار موبد is the object 'the mobed's work/duties'. نگاه in this position has the extended sense 'to keep/tend/perform' (literal sense 'look' is extended to mean 'attend to' in context). The clause therefore gives the purpose of sending: 'so that he might perform the mobed's work.'
segments.s664-g229: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause ('for/that'). آن زهر 'that poison' is the subject; شد is the past copula 'became' and forms 'became effective'. بر تنش is a prepositional phrase 'upon his body' with the enclitic ش 'his'. کارگر is a predicative adjective here meaning 'effective' or 'operative'. The clause thus states that the poison took effect on his body.
segments.s664-g230: گر is the conditional 'if'. اندیشه ما نیامد literally 'our thought did not come' is an idiomatic way to say 'if our plan/advice does not occur or succeed' (نیامد is past negative 'did not come/arrive'). ببر is an imperative 'take!' (addressed to an understood second person), here meaning 'take him away' or 'carry him off'.
segments.s664-g231: فرستاده را uses the object marker را ('the messenger' as definite object). چشم موبد literally 'the eye of the mobed' functions as the grammatical subject/agent of بدید ('saw') — a possessive noun phrase preceding the verb is common in classical Persian. So the clause reads 'the mobed saw the messenger' (lit. 'the messenger the mobed's eye saw').
segments.s664-g232: سرشکش ('a small drop, teardrop') is the subject; ز مژگان ('from the eyelashes') marks the source; به رخ ('onto the cheek/face') is the goal; برچکید is a perfective/past verb 'fell/dripped' (in poetic use it carries the sense 'began to drip' or 'burst forth'). The whole line describes a tear falling from his lashes onto his cheek.
segments.s664-g105: چو here is the comparative/relational marker 'as' (not temporal), introducing the names that follow. The phrase lists two personal names joined by و; دگر before برزمهر means 'the other' (i.e. the second of the pair). The segment is nominal and has no finite verb; it attaches as an apposition to the next segment.
segments.s664-g106: This is a nominal description: دبیر (noun) modified by the adjective خردمند, followed by a prepositional phrase با + (nouns) indicating accompaniment/possession. فر and چهر are coordinated by و; با governs both and describes the scribe's attributes ('with farr and countenance'). The whole segment completes the appositive begun in the previous segment.
segments.s664-g107: سه دیگر is a counted noun phrase 'three others.' که introduces a relative clause: ماه‌آذرش بود نام literally 'his Mah‑Azar was name' → 'whose name was Mah‑Azar.' The clitic -ش on ماه‌آذر marks the possessive 'his'; بود is the past copula placed before the predicate (poetic word order).
segments.s664-g108: A string of three coordinated adjectives (خردمند و روشن‌دل و شادکام) post‑modifying the person named; each adjective is linked by و and all qualify the antecedent inside the relative clause. No copula appears here because the adjectives continue the predication begun with بود in the previous segment.
segments.s664-g109: بر تخت نوشین‌روان is a locative prepositional phrase 'on the throne of Nushin‑ravan' (preposition بر + noun + epithet). این سه پیر is the demonstrative + numeral + noun 'these three elders' and functions as the subject; the phrase order (locative fronted) is idiomatic in epic/persian verse.
segments.s664-g110: چو is 'as'/'like' introducing a predicative similitude. دستور functions here as a predicative noun ('organisers/instructors/officials' in context) and بودند is the past‑plural copula 'they were.' The conjunction و links a second simile 'همچون وزیر' ('and like viziers'), so the clause says they held the role (were) as commanders/organisers and resembled viziers.
segments.s664-g111: همی‌خواست is an older/perfective‑imperfective verb form meaning 'was wishing/was about to' (a habitual or intended action in the past). هرمز is the subject. کزین (= از + این) marks origin 'from these,' so کزین هرسه مرد = 'from these three men,' the object of his intended action.
segments.s664-g112: یکایک 'one by one' is an adverbial quantifier; برآرد is a present/future‑oriented classical form 'bring forth / take up' (here: 'remove/raise/bring' each person). به ناگاه is an adverbial phrase 'suddenly,' and گرد functions adverbially/elliptically to convey togetherness or turning (literary 'about/round'); the whole string describes the manner/timing of his intended action: to bring each in turn, suddenly.
segments.s664-g169: بدان is an adverbial/Prepositional idea here (literally be- + دان) meaning 'to/for that' or 'thereupon'; رنج is the noun 'hardship/suffering'; پاداش is the head noun 'reward/recompense'; بند is used in the sense 'fetter/bond' (a metaphorical result); آمدست is an archaic past/perfect-form (compound older Persian) meaning 'has come' or 'has come to be'. Word order is literary: prepositional phrase (بدان رنج) → topic (پاداش) → predicate noun (بند) → verbal predicate (آمدست).
segments.s664-g170: پس از introduces a temporal/causal clause 'after' (پس از = 'after/of'); رنج is again 'hardship/suffering'; بیم گزند is a compounded noun phrase 'fear (بیم) of harm (گزند)'; آمدست is the same archaic past 'has come'. The whole clause means 'after the suffering, the fear of harm came.'
segments.s664-g171: This is a descriptive phrase addressing the king: دلی is a nominal 'a heart' (here used as the speaker's heart); بیگنه = بیگانه/بیگانه‌گونه 'strange, unfamiliar'; پرغم‌ای is پرغم + the vocative clitic ای: 'full of sorrow, O ...'; شهریار is the vocative 'O king.' Word order is poetic and compact: 'a heart, strange and full of sorrow—O king.'
segments.s664-g172: به یزدان = 'to God' (به + یزدان). نمایم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive/intentional form of نمايـدن 'to show/present' meaning 'I present / I show'. به‌روز شمار is a phrase 'by the day / counting days' (به‌روز 'by day' + شمار 'count'), so the clause says 'I present (it) to God and count the days' (or 'I present it to God, counting the days').
segments.s664-g173: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; موبد is the subject 'mōbed' (Zoroastrian priest); سوی خانه شد is 'turned/moved toward the house' (سوی = 'toward', خانه = 'house', شد an older past marker meaning 'turned/went'). در زمان here adds 'at that time / in that moment.' The order is typical epic style: temporal conjunction → subject → motion toward place → temporal phrase.
segments.s664-g174: ز (poetic form of از) = 'from'; کارآگهان (کارآگه) is an adjective/noun 'those who are aware/informed' or 'an informed one'; رفت is the past 'went/left'; مردی = 'a man'; دمان (here) literally 'hem/skirt/edge'—in context it marks place or fringe where the man moved. The line means roughly 'from the informed one(s) a man went off at the hem/edge' (i.e. an aware man slipped away).
segments.s664-g175: شنیده is the perfect/participial form 'having heard'; یکایک = 'one by one' / 'each and every'; بهرمزد here is a proper name (Bahramzad) in the narrative, not the lexical sense 'for reward'; گفت is the past of گفتن 'said' (here the simple past 'he said'). Grammatically this is a temporal/background participle ('having heard ... he said').
segments.s664-g176: دل شاه = 'the king's heart' (possessive noun phrase). با رای بد = 'with bad counsel' (با = 'with', رای = 'opinion/counsel/plan', بد = 'bad/evil'). گشت is past 'became' or 'turned' (گشت = became/was turned); جفت = 'paired/joined' (here 'became joined/paired with [that counsel]'). So the clause means 'the king's heart, with bad counsel, became joined/was swayed.'
segments.s664-g65: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. شاه از تو خشنود شد is a full clause in past tense: شاه (subject) + از تو (prepositional complement 'with you') + خشنود شد (khushnūd + شد 'became/was pleased'); راستیست follows as the main comment/predication ('it is true').
segments.s664-g66: This clause is a conditional/critical comment connected to the previous: وزو is an archaic contraction (functioning like 'وز او' = 'from him/of him') followed by سر (object) and the present/subjunctive-form verb بپیچی 'you turn/bow' (used here as the protasis/condition). در introduces the predicative phrase and کاستیست (nominal predicate) means 'it is a defect' or 'there is a shortcoming.'
segments.s664-g67: This is a nominal sentence where درشتیش (his roughness/bluntness, with possessive -ش) is the subject and نرمیست is the predicate ('is softness'). در پند تو is a locative/relational phrase 'in your counsel' modifying the predicate — stylistically paradoxical: 'his harshness counts as gentleness in your advice.'
segments.s664-g68: بجوید is a present-tense/imperative-like or narrative verb 'he/one seeks' (to find out). The conjunction که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/why' in which شد is past 'became' and گرم is used figuratively 'fervent/strong' modifying پیوند تو ('your bond'). Together: 'he seeks (to know) why your bond became fervent.'
segments.s664-g69: This is an admonition using the negative imperative مپرهیز 'do not refrain/avoid.' ز نیکی 'from good/deed' is the object/goal (i.e., 'do not refrain from doing good'), هرگز 'never' strengthens the prohibition, and به رنج is an adverbial phrase 'for the sake of hardship' meaning 'even if it causes you hardship.'
segments.s664-g70: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not (make/let).' The phrase شادمان دل is a quasi-objective expression 'make (your) heart glad' (شادمان modifying the heart), and به بیداد گنج is a prepositional phrase 'at/over the treasure of injustice' — i.e., 'do not let your heart be glad about ill-gotten treasure.'
segments.s664-g71: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. اندر جهان (in the world / in this life) sets the domain; کام دل 'the desire of the heart' is the object of یافتی, which is the second-person past 'you obtained/attained' — 'when you obtained the desire of your heart.'
segments.s664-g72: رسیدی is past 'you arrived/reached' and بجایی is an adverbial/complement meaning 'to a place/state' (here 'to that place/condition'); که introduces a relative/subordinate clause clarifying which place, and بشتافتی is past second-person 'you hastened' — together: 'you reached the place to which you had hastened.'
segments.s664-g361: که here is a relative pronoun ('who/that') linking back to the previously mentioned person. بهرام را is the object (proper name + the accusative marker را); Persian order places the object before the verb. پیش شاه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of the king' (پیش = 'before/in front of/in the presence of'). آورد is a past-tense 3rd-person singular verb 'brought.'
segments.s664-g362: بدان is a contracted locative: the preposition ب(ـه) 'to/into' + an archaic demonstrative 'دان' (= 'that'), so 'to that.' نامور بارگاه is a noun phrase 'renowned court/palace' (adjective + noun). آورد repeats the verb 'brought' and completes the idea 'brought (him) to that famed court' (the object 'him' is understood from the previous clause).
segments.s664-g363: شب تیره is an adverbial time phrase 'on a dark night' (noun + adjective). بهرام را is again the object marked by را. پیش here means 'before/in the presence of' (پیش + شاه implied 'before him'). خواند is the past 3rd-person verb 'called/summoned' (here 'summoned him').
segments.s664-g364: به is used adverbially to indicate manner ('by/with' — 'with oily speech'). چربی سخن is a metaphoric expression 'oily/fat speech' = flattering language (چربی literally 'fat/grease' modifying سخن 'speech'). چند here means 'a few / some' (qualifying 'words'). با او 'to/with him' marks the addressee. براند is a 3rd-person verb form from راندن ('to drive/urge/say' in older usage) here functioning as 'addressed/spoke to' — narrative verb at the clause end.
segments.s664-g365: بدو is an enclitic/dative 'to him.' گفت here introduces reported speech ('he said'); the printed senses list did not include the verbal 'said' sense, so that entry is irregular in the provided senses. برگوی is an imperative 'say / tell' (the verb used to introduce the content of the speech). کان here is an archaic/deictic element ('that' / 'the said') used to point to 'that advice' (not reflected in the provided senses). پند چیست is the quoted question 'what is the advice?' with چی 'what' and ست (short for 'است') the copula 'is.'
segments.s664-g366: که introduces a subordinate clause (here continuing the question). ما را uses ما 'we/us' plus را (here topical/dative/emphatic). بدان is again ب(ـه) + archaic دان = 'to/for that' (i.e. 'at that [time]'). روزگار 'time/age' names the temporal frame. بهیست (ب + هست → بهیست) is an archaic fused copular phrase meaning 'it is fitting / is proper.'
segments.s664-g367: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase 'thus gave (a) reply' — چنین 'thus', داد 'gave' (past 3rd person), پاسخ 'answer.' که begins the content clause ('that/in which/that...'). در گنج شاه is a locative phrase 'in the king's treasury' (در = 'in', گنج = 'treasure/treasury', شاه = 'king').
segments.s664-g368: یکی here means 'a certain' (indefinite 'one' = 'a certain one'). ساده صندوق is a noun phrase 'a simple/plain chest' (adjective + noun). دیدم is the speaker's verb 'I saw' (past 1st-person). سیاه 'black' describes the chest; placed after the verb it functions as a final descriptive adjective, typical of poetic word order.
segments.s664-g57: وگر is the conditional conjunction 'if'. شهریارت is شهریار + the possessive suffix -ت (= 'your king'). بود is the past/copular verb 'was/were' used here in a conditional sense ('were'); دادگر is a predicate adjective 'just'. The order is subject (شهریارت) + copula (بود) + predicate (دادگر), a common poetic inversion.
segments.s664-g58: تو is the emphatic second‑person pronoun 'you'. بر وی is a prepositional phrase 'on/upon him' (i.e. 'in him' / 'relying on him'). بسستی is an archaic/poetic verbal form here meaning roughly 'to rely / be secure' (not the more common modern forms), and گمانی مبر is a prohibitive: گمانی 'a supposition' + مبر (imperative negative 'do not take'). Together this clause issues the warning 'do not suppose you can rely on him.'
segments.s664-g59: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایددون is a proper name (the person being spoken of). که here introduces a subordinate clause ('that' / 'who'). گویی is a poetic/older verbal form related to 'to say/appear' and in this construction functions within the subordinate clause meaning roughly 'that you (might) [be thought to] ...'; نداند is third‑person negative 'does not know'. همی is an old poetic particle giving aspect/emphasis (often progressive or emphatic). The whole segment sets up the supposition about Eiddūn's opinion.
segments.s664-g60: سخن‌های شاهان is a genitive phrase 'the words/sayings of the kings' (سخن‌های = 'words/speeches'). بخواند is a verbal form meaning 'he will read/recite' (classical narrative present/future sense), and the final همی again marks the habitual/progressive aspect. Together: 'he will (still/next) recite the kings' words.'
segments.s664-g61: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بخشایش 'mercy/forgiveness' is the object; از دل کند is a light‑verb construction 'takes away from the heart' (از + دل = 'from the heart', کند = 'does/takes'). شهریار at the end is the subject 'the king' — this SOV ordering (object + verb + subject) is common in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s664-g62: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. اندر زمین is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the land'. تخم کژی is a noun + adjective 'the seed of crookedness' (i.e. the origin/fruit of wrongdoing). مکار is an epithet/address 'deceitful one' or an adjectival predicate; here it functions as an accusation directed at 'you'.
segments.s664-g63: هرآنکس 'whoever' begins the relative clause; که is the subordinator 'who/that'. او is the third‑person pronoun 'he'. پند ما داشت is a past construction 'had/took our advice' (داشت = past 'had'). خوار is a predicative adjective 'despised' or 'held cheap' applied to that person. Word order follows classical patterns: relative + object/verb + predicate.
segments.s664-g64: بشوید is the impersonal/third‑person form 'becomes / is made' (here 'becomes bereft'). دل is 'the heart'. از خوبی روزگار is a prepositional phrase 'from the goodness of the times/fortune' — here meaning 'deprived of life's good/fortune'. Together the clause says the person's heart is left without the good things of fate.
segments.s664-g33: به is the preposition 'to/for'; درویش is the noun 'dervish/poor person' and is the indirect object of the action; بر + مهربانی forms a poetic locution meaning 'in/with kindness' (بر here marks the sphere/way of action); کنیم is the 1st-person-plural present-subjunctive/hortative of کردن meaning 'let us do' (here a hortative: 'let us show'). Word order is typical poetic Persian: preposition + object + phrase + verb.
segments.s664-g34: به introduces the beneficiary/target again ('to/for'); پرمایه here denotes those of substance/wealth (poetic adjective); بر پاسبانی is a nominal phrase 'in/for guarding' or 'as guardians' where بر marks the manner/role; کنیم is the same hortative 'let us do' — taken together: 'let us act as guards/protectors for the substantial/wealthy.'
segments.s664-g35: هرآن‌کس 'whoever' begins a relative clause introduced by که; ایمن شد is the past tense 'became safe/was secure' (شد = past of شدن); از کار خویش literally 'from/of his own affairs' — خویش is expressed by خوی + enclitic ش (see next word) meaning 'his own'. The clause means 'whoever was secure about his affairs.'
segments.s664-g36: بر ما 'toward us' marks the target; چنان کرد is a past construction where چنان 'thus/so' modifies کرد 'he did/made' (so he acted); بازار خویش 'his market' is the object phrase (بازار = market; خویش = his own, formed by خوی + enclitic ش). Poetic word order places the target first (بر ما) and the possessor after بازار.
segments.s664-g37: شما را topicalizes 'you' with the object marker را; به من 'to me' marks the addressee; هرچ هست literally 'whatever there is' qualifies آرزوی 'wish/desire' — here آرزوی is a noun meaning 'wish' and the sequence as a whole is an offer: 'whatever you wish of me.' The verb that governs this noun is supplied in the following clause (آرزوی ... مدارید).
segments.s664-g38: مدارید is an exhortative/imperative-form verb meaning here roughly 'do not restrain yourselves / be liberal' (root related to مدارا; in this context it invites the addressees to express their wishes). راز 'secret' with از دل نیک‌خوی 'from the kind-hearted heart' is the object phrase: 'pour out / reveal the secret of a good/noble heart.' In sum, the line encourages asking freely and laying open the kind-hearted secret.
segments.s664-g39: ز is the poetic form of از 'from'; چیزی 'something' is the head of the relative clause introduced by که; دلتان 'your heart' uses the possessive clitic -تان; هراسان بود is past-tense compound 'was frightened' (هراسان adjective + بود 'was'). The clause therefore means 'from that thing which had made your heart fearful.'
segments.s664-g40: مرا را (here split in the edition as م + را) is the indirect-object 'to me'; داد is the simple past 'gave'; آن دادن is a nominal phrase 'that giving' (آن = 'that' + دادن = gerund/infinitive 'giving'); آسان بود is the copular past 'was easy.' The second clause comments on the first: 'He gave it to me — that giving was easy.' Note: the edition shows م as a separate token; functionally it is the 'me' of مرا.
segments.s664-g345: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' (poetic). 'بهرام آذرمهان' is a compound proper name (subject) placed before the verb. آن here is a demonstrative/object pronoun ('that, it') referring to the news; شنید is a simple past verb 'heard'. The whole segment = 'When Bahram Azarmahan heard that...'.
segments.s664-g346: که introduces the reported clause ('that'). آن again points to the person being spoken of ('that/the'). پاکدل is a compound adjective 'pure‑hearted'; مرد is the noun 'man/person'. شد is the past of شدن used here as a copula/auxiliary 'became/was', and ناپدید is a predicative adjective 'vanished/gone'. Literally: 'that the pure‑hearted man became vanished' → 'that the pure‑hearted man had vanished.'
segments.s664-g347: پیامی is the indefinite direct object 'a message'; in Persian the object can precede the verb. فرستاد is the past tense 'sent'. نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'near/to the king' meaning 'to the king's presence' (location/recipient).
segments.s664-g348: که introduces the content of the message (reported speech). The surface 'که‌ای' reflects that introduction plus a vocative 'ای' (= 'O'); تاج تو is the noun phrase 'your crown' (possessed by تو). برتر از is the comparative 'higher/superior than' and چرخ ماه is a poetic genitive 'the wheel/sphere of the moon' (چرخ = celestial sphere/sky). Word order: Subject (تاج تو) + predicate (برتر) + comparison (از ...).
segments.s664-g349: تو دانی که = 'you know that'. من چند کوشیده‌ام places the adverb/quantifier چند ('how much/how long') before the perfect verb; کوشیده‌ام is present perfect 'I have striven/tried'. The clause states the extent of the speaker's effort.
segments.s664-g350: که again introduces the subordinate/result clause. تا here functions as a purposive/resultive marker ('so that/in order/resulting in') linking the effort to its outcome. رازهای تو is the object 'your secrets' and پوشیده‌ام is perfect 'I have kept/concealed' (پوشیدن = to cover/conceal). Together: 'that so (by my trying) I have kept your secrets concealed.'
segments.s664-g351: به پیش پدرت = 'in the presence of/ before your father' (به + پیش + پدر + 2nd‑person suffix ت). آن is a demonstrative/emphatic particle pointing to the following predicate. سزاوار شاه is a predicate phrase 'worthy of the king/throne' (سزاوار + شاه). The whole segment states location/context + the quality evaluated.
segments.s664-g352: نبودم is the negative past 'I was not'. تو را uses را as a dative/benefactive marker here ('for you' / 'toward you'). جز means 'except' and همه نیکخواه functions as 'nothing but a (sincere) well‑wisher' (همه as an intensifier/collective). The clause means 'I was, for you, nothing except a well‑wisher.'
segments.s664-g289: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when'. The phrase places the proper name 'بهرام آذرمهان' immediately after it; 'پیشرو' is a descriptive participle/adjective 'leading, advancing' attached to the name. The clause is elliptical (no explicit copula) and functions as a temporal subordinate: 'When Bahram Azarmahān (was) at the front...'.
segments.s664-g290: The initial چو here is a simile marker 'like/as'. 'سیمای برزین' is a genitive construction ('the visage/appearance of Barzin'), and the conjunction و links that image to 'گردان نو' ('a new/fresh troop'). Word order is noun + modifier: 'گردان نو' = 'new troop'.
segments.s664-g291: نشستند is a past plural verb 'they sat / they took their seats' (here the supplied lemma/senses are for شستن and do not match meaning, so the correct sense is not in the provided list). هریک 'each (one)' is the distributive, and به آیین خویش means 'according to their own custom' (به + noun used adverbially = 'in the manner of'; خوی + enclitic ش = 'their').
segments.s664-g292: گروهی here functions as an indefinite quantifier 'a number/some (of them)'. ببودند is an archaic/past form meaning 'they were' (past copula). بر پای پیش is an idiomatic locative: بر + پای پیش = 'at the fore / in the foremost place' (literal 'on the front foot').
segments.s664-g293: به ... گفت شاه = 'the king said to Bahram': the prepositional indirect object 'به بهرام آذرمهان' precedes گفت, and شاه (the subject) follows—this word order (indirect object + گفت + subject) is a common poetic/older Persian ordering. Note: the provided senses for گفت are for a noun form and do not include the verb 'said', so the correct verbal sense is not in the supplied list.
segments.s664-g294: که introduces reported content ('that ...'). سیمای برزین is the genitive 'Barzin's visage/appearance'. بدین is tokenized here as ب + دین but functions as 'in/at this' (به + این); because the tokens provided don't match this usage (دین's listed senses are unrelated) the token 'دین' cannot be matched to a supplied sense. بارگاه = 'royal court/palace'.
segments.s664-g295: سزاوار گنجست is a predicative phrase 'is deserving/worthy of treasure' (سزاوار = deserving; گنجست = 'is treasure/worthy of treasure' — an older/poetic copular construction). اگر مرد رنج is a conditional 'if (he is) a man of toil/hardship' where 'مرد رنج' is a compound meaning 'man of suffering/one who endures hardship'.
segments.s664-g296: که here gives a reason/explanation ('for' / 'because'). بدخواه = 'ill-wisher / malevolent person'. زیبا is 'beautiful' (used predicatively), and نباشد is a negative present/subjunctive form 'is not' / 'would not be' (the supplied senses for نباشد do not match this verbal use, so the correct verbal sense is not in the provided list). به گنج = 'by/with treasure' (instrumental/associative).
segments.s664-g121: 'ازان' is the poetic form of 'ازِ آن' (= 'from that') and starts a prepositional phrase 'ازان بند' = 'from that stanza/verse'. 'ایزدگشسب دبیر' is an apposition: the proper name followed by the title 'dābir/secretary, scribe' (name + role), and this whole noun phrase is the subject of the following predicate 'چنان شد'.
segments.s664-g122: 'چنان شد که' is a result clause meaning 'became such that/so that'. 'دل‌خسته' is a compound adjective 'heart‑sore / sorrowful', and 'گردد' is the literary/optative/present form of 'گشتن' used inside the result clause ('would / came to be'); 'به تیر' gives the cause/means ('by/through an arrow'). The clause thus expresses the result (heart‑soreness) as caused by an arrow.
segments.s664-g123: چو روزی برآمد = 'when a day dawned/when a day came'. The verb phrase 'نبودش زوار' literally 'there were no visitors for him' uses the clitic '-ش' (for him) attached to the negative copula 'نبود' and places the noun 'زوار' (visitors/pilgrims) after it — a common inverted order in classical Persian.
segments.s664-g124: The triple negation 'نه X و نه Y نه Z' lists things he lacked; the verb 'نبود' from the previous segment is understood to govern all three. 'خورد' here functions nominally ('food' / things to eat), 'پوشش' = 'cloak/covering', and 'انده‌گسار' = 'comforter / solace'. The coordinating 'و' links the first two items.
segments.s664-g125: 'ز زندان' = 'from prison' (prepositional place/source). 'پیامی فرستاد' is the past action 'sent a message'. 'دوست' is the agent ('the friend') and appears after the verb by literary word order (V ... S inversion is common in epic diction).
segments.s664-g126: 'به موبد' = 'to the mobed/priest' (indirect object). The form 'که‌ای' here is the classical sequence 'که ای' collapsed in verse: 'که' introducing reported speech plus the vocative 'ای' ('O ...'), marking the start of the direct address. 'بنده را مغز و پوست' is the opening of that reported speech: 'bande (this humble self) + را' (object/topic marker) followed by the two nouns 'مغز' (brain/core) and 'پوست' (skin), i.e. the things being spoken about.
segments.s664-g127: 'منم' is emphatic 'I am' (subject + clitic). 'بی‌زواری' is an adjective/nominal with the negative prefix 'بی-' meaning 'without ...', here best read 'without complaint / without desire to complain' describing the speaker's condition. 'به زندان شاه' is a locative phrase 'in/at the king's prison' (classical 'به' + place can indicate being in or at).
segments.s664-g128: This line uses inverted/orderly classical phrasing: 'کسی را' (anyone, with the object/topic marker) + 'به نزدیک من' ('to/near me') + 'نیست راه' = literally 'there is no way' — i.e. 'there is no way for anyone to come near me.' The existential negation 'نیست' negates the noun 'راه' ('way'), with the movement/approach expressed by 'به نزدیک من'.
segments.s664-g73: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دیهیم here refers collectively to the rural people / villagers (classical Persian often uses a singular form for a collective group). هفتاد‌بر is an adverbial degree 'seventyfold' modifying the verb phrase that follows. سرنهی is the verbal phrase 'سر نهی' (written together here for poetic compactness) meaning 'raise (one's) head' — the verb is in a present/gnomic form describing what happens at that time.
segments.s664-g74: همه 'everyone' is the group affected. گرد کرده is a compound verbal expression: گرد (verb 'to gather/turn about') with کرده as the light/perfective auxiliary/participle, together meaning 'having gathered' or 'make gather'. به دشمن marks the target/against whom the action is directed ('toward/against the enemy'). دهی is the second‑person present/optative causative 'you make/give/bring about' — in context the speaker addresses an addressee who causes everyone to be turned against the enemy.
segments.s664-g75: بهر کار is a prepositional phrase 'for / with respect to the affair' (بهر = 'for', کار = 'affair/work'). درویش is the person concerned ('dervish' / ascetic). دارد دلم is a poetic inversion: the verb دارد ('has') appears before the possessed nominal دلم ('my heart' = دل + enclitic -م). Literally 'has my heart' → idiomatically 'my heart cares / my heart has concern'.
segments.s664-g76: نخواهم is the negative desiderative 'I do not wish / I will not want'. که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). اندیشه is the object 'thought / intention'. ز + و here form the classical contracted prepositional phrase 'زو' = از + او = 'from him/of him' (ز = 'from', و here stands for the pronoun 'او'); in the word list they are split but together mean 'from him'. بگسلم is the present/optative form with the prefix بـ- marking the subjunctive/intent ('that I should break/sever') from the root گسل/گسستن — so the clause means 'I do not wish that I sever my thought from him.'
segments.s664-g77: همی‌خواهم is a present/desiderative 'I wish / I ask' (the prefixed همی- gives a continuous or earnest present). از introduces the source 'from', پاک is the attributive adjective 'pure', and پروردگار is the noun 'Lord / Sustainer' — together 'from the Pure Lord' is the object/source of the speaker's petition.
segments.s664-g78: که introduces the content/purpose clause ('that ...'). چندان is an adverb 'so much / to that extent'. م + را should be read together as مرا (me) + the object marker را (the listing splits them): this is the first‑person object 'me' (مرا). بر دهد روزگار is a light-clause: بر here has the sense 'upon / to' and دهد is a third‑person present/optative 'may give / give' — روزگار 'fate / fortune' is the subject of the giving. The whole clause: 'that fate give me so much.'
segments.s664-g79: که again introduces purpose/result ('so that'). درویش را is the marked direct object 'the dervish' (را = definite object marker). شاد دارم is a causative/perfective idiom: شاد (happy) plus the light verb دارم 'I keep/make (someone) ...', so 'I keep the dervish happy.' به گنج 'with/through treasure' indicates the means/instrument ('by giving treasure').
segments.s664-g80: نیارم is a negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'I will not bring / I will not cause' (classical contraction of نیاورم / 'I do not cause/produce'), so it negates the following action. دل پارسا را is the object 'the pious one's heart' (دل = heart, پارسا = pious person, را = object marker). به رنج 'into suffering / to hardship' is the result/condition to be avoided. Together: 'I will not cause the pious man's heart to come to suffering.'
segments.s664-g97: برآشفت is a past third-person form meaning ‘became angry’ (verb + prefix), and و simply coordinates the next phrase. خوی بد آورد is a light-verb construction: خوی (disposition) + adjective بد (bad) + آورد (lit. ‘brought/took on’), so together ‘assumed a bad disposition.’ پیش is an adverb/post-position meaning ‘before/in the presence of’ and follows the verb to mark the audience/location.
segments.s664-g98: به یک‌سو شد is a compound: به (towards/into) + یک‌سو (‘one side/aside’) + شد (past of شدن ‘become’) → ‘turned aside.’ از راه آیین و کیش is a prepositional phrase with از marking separation (‘from/out of’), راه ‘way/manner’, آیین ‘custom/ritual’ and کیش ‘creed/sect’ — together ‘from the way of ritual and creed.’
segments.s664-g99: هرآن‌کس is a generalizing relative pronoun ‘whoever.’ که introduces the relative clause. نزد پدرش = نزد (in the sight/presence of) + پدرش (his father) and functions as a prepositional complement. ارجمند is the predicate adjective ‘esteemed/valued’ describing that person in his father’s eyes.
segments.s664-g100: This verbless phrase strings adjectives and noun-phrases: بدی is literally ‘evil/harm’ (here part of a compressed idiom), شاد ‘glad/joyful,’ و ‘and,’ ایمن ‘safe/protected’; ز (poetic form of از) means ‘from’; and بیم گزند is a compound ‘fear of harm’ (بیم ‘fear’ + گزند ‘harm’). The line reads idiomatically as ‘was joyful and safe from fear of harm,’ though the poetical ordering compresses the elements.
segments.s664-g101: یکایک is an adverb meaning ‘one by one’ or ‘each in turn.’ تبه کردشان is a verbal phrase: تبه کردن ‘to ruin / make wicked’ + کرد (past) with the pronominal clitic شان ‘them’ attached, so ‘he ruined them.’ بی‌گناه is a postpositive adjective meaning ‘innocent’ that qualifies the object.
segments.s664-g102: بدین گونه is the adverbial phrase ‘in this way/ thus’ (here written with the elements split: ب + دین + گونه), where گونه ‘manner’ is the head. بد رای is an adjective-noun sequence ‘bad + رای (opinion/plan)’ meaning ‘ill counsel/ill judgment.’ و آیین شاه = ‘and the king’s آیین (custom/practice)’ (possessor follows the noun in Persian), so the whole phrase gives the cause: ‘by the king’s bad counsel and practice.’
segments.s664-g103: This is a noun phrase: 'سه' (three) numerally modifies 'مرد' (man), and 'از دبیران' means 'from/among the secretaries' (partitive/source). 'نوشین‌روان' is an epithet ('sweet‑souled' or 'pleasant‑minded') qualifying 'دبیران' and is placed after it in poetic style; the ezafe linking them is omitted in verse. Note Persian allows the singular noun 'مرد' after a numeral.
segments.s664-g104: 'یکی پیر و دانا و دیگر جوان' is a coordinated predication with an omitted copula (بود). 'یکی' introduces one of the three ('one'), followed by two predicative adjectives 'پیر و دانا' ('old and wise') joined by 'و'. After another 'و' the phrase 'دیگر جوان' means 'the other (was) young' — 'دیگر' = 'another/the other' plus 'جوان' as predicate; the verb 'was' is left out, typical in Persian nominal/poetic lists.
segments.s664-g329: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the clause. این 'this' + ترک‌زاده 'Turk-born / of Turkic descent' form the subject, followed by the predicate سزاوار نیست where سزاوار is an adjective 'fit/appropriate' and نیست is the negative copula 'is not'. The clause thus states 'that this Turk‑born is not fit.'
segments.s664-g330: به شاهی is a prepositional phrase 'for/to kingship' (به + شاهی). کس here functions as 'anyone/someone' and together with the final negative نیست yields an existential negative reading ('there is no one'). او را is the definite object with را marking 'him', and خریدار is used predicatively ('buyer / taker'); the whole phrase reads literally 'for kingship someone to buy him there is not' → 'no one will buy / accept him as king.'
segments.s664-g331: که 'that' introduces the clause; خاقان نژادست is a nominal predicate where نژادست is a poetic/compound form ('is of lineage') meaning 'is of khagan birth'. The conjunction و links the second predicate بد‌گوهرست ('is of bad or base stock') — both predicates use the fused copular form -ست common in old/poetic Persian.
segments.s664-g332: به بالا و دیدار is a coordinated phrase with به introducing two spheres: بالا ('height / highness / stature') and دیدار (here 'appearance / how one is seen' — sense not listed in the provided options). چون مادرست is a simile: چون 'like/as' + مادرست ('is like a mother' / 'motherly'); overall: 'in height and appearance he is like his mother.'
segments.s664-g333: تو 'you' is the subject; گفتی would normally be the past-tense verb 'you said' (here the supplied senses list does not match that form, so no sense index). که introduces the reported content 'that Hormuz deserves kingship'; هرمز is a personal name here, به شاهی 'to the kingship' marks the domain, and سزاست is the adjectival/copular form 'it is deserved / he deserves.'
segments.s664-g334: کنون 'now' starts the second clause. زین سزا is an idiomatic phrase 'for/from this desert/reward (or punishment)' — زین here functions deictically ('from/for this') though the exact 'from/this' sense wasn't in the supplied list. مر is the dative/clitic 'to me' and تو را has the object/topic marker 'to you'; the final این جزاست literally 'this is the recompense' (جزا/جزاست = 'recompense/punishment'). The clause means 'now, for that deed/advice, this is my recompense for you.'
segments.s664-g335: گوایی is a parenthetical adverb 'apparently / it seems'; من is the subject 'I'. از بهر این is a purposive phrase ('for this reason' — از here functions in the idiom از بهر = 'for, because of'), and دادمت is the compound past verb 'I gave you' with the enclitic object -ت 'you' and first-person marker -م 'I' (I gave you).
segments.s664-g336: چنین 'thus/so' modifies the following idiom لب به دشنام بگشادمت: لب 'lip' + به دشنام 'to (an) insult' and بگشادمت is a past-form verb meaning 'I opened / I uttered' with the clitic indicating the object (you). The whole phrase is the set expression 'to open one's lips to curse/insult' → 'I opened my lips in insult against you.'
segments.s664-g113: همی‌بود is a literary imperfective past meaning “kept being / remained.” ز ایشان expresses cause (“because of them”), and دلش پرهراس is a predicate nominal phrase literally “his heart (دلش) [was] fearful (پرهراس).” The verb (همی‌بود) appears before the cause phrase and predicate because of poetic inversion.
segments.s664-g114: که introduces a subordinate clause of feared possibility (“that / that…may”). روزی here is an adverbial ‘one day / someday’ (note: the supplied sense list does not include this adverbial sense). شوند is the subjunctive/potential form of شدن meaning “become,” اندرو means “in/against him / toward him,” and ناسپاس is the predicate adjective “ungrateful.”
segments.s664-g115: The phrase is temporal + action: آن زمان “at that time.” The verb phrase دست آخت (literary) means “laid hands on / attacked / set upon.” به + name (به ایزدگشسب) marks the object/person affected. The verb is poetic/archaic here, so its precise sense is irregular.
segments.s664-g116: به بیهوده is an adverbial phrase “in vain / needlessly” (به used adverbially). بر بند و زندانش is the object phrase ‘onto fetters and his prison’ (بند = fetter, زندانش = his prison), and ساخت is a past verb “made/did” meaning here “he bound him / put him in prison.” Word order places the adverbial and object before the verb under poetic style.
segments.s664-g117: دل موبد موبدان is a genitive/appositional phrase meaning “the heart of the mobed of mobeds” (chief priest). تنگ شد is a simple past predicate “became tight / constricted,” used idiomatically for distress or trouble. The structure is noun phrase + predicate (subject then verb) in plain past.
segments.s664-g118: رخانش is the personal-form noun “his visage/appearance” (poetic/archaic formation). ز اندیشه = “from/because of thought (worry),” and بی‌رنگ شد is “became colorless / pale.” The preposition ز marks cause and شد is the past-tense copula “became.”
segments.s664-g119: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause “that/for,” موبد is the subject, and بد و پاک are two coordinated adjectives describing سرشت (nature/disposition). بودش is the past tense of بودن with the enclitic pronoun ش = “his/it,” so the clause says literally “his nature was bad and pure.” The phrasing is compact and characteristic of epic diction.
segments.s664-g120: بمردی is an adverbial “by/for manliness / for his bravery” that gives the reason. ورا = “to him,” نام = “name” (here a bestowed epithet), بد attaches as part of that epithet (“Bad-…”), and زردهشت is a proper name (Zardešt/Zoroaster used as a name element). The clause is elliptical (the verb ‘was given’ or ‘they called him’ is implied rather than explicit).
segments.s664-g137: ز پیغام او is a prepositional phrase meaning “because of/from his message” (ز = because of). شد is the past copular verb 'became'; دلش is the noun دل 'heart' with the enclitic possessive -ش 'his'. پرشکن is a predicate adjective following شد: word order here is [prepositional phrase] + شد + subject+possessive + predicate, giving “because of his message his heart became broken/shattered.”
segments.s664-g138: پراندیشه is a predicative adjective meaning 'full of thought / pensive'; شد is again the past copula 'became'. مغزش is مغز 'brain' with the possessive -ش 'his' and functions as the grammatical subject. از خویشتن is a prepositional phrase 'out of himself / away from himself' modifying the state. Overall: 'his brain became full of thought, he was beside himself.'
segments.s664-g139: به زندان is a directional prepositional phrase 'to/into the prison' (به = to/into). فرستاد is the past transitive 'sent'. لختی here means 'a little / a small portion' and خورش is 'stew/food'; together they form the direct object placed after the verb (verb + object), i.e. 'sent a small portion of stew to the prison.'
segments.s664-g140: بلرزید is a simple past intransitive 'he/it trembled'. زان کار is a causal prepositional phrase 'because of that deed/act' (زان = from/for that; کار = deed). دل در برش is an idiomatic locative: دل 'heart' + در 'in' + برش (here a pronominal/locative element) to mean 'his heart trembled in him / in his breast.' The clause thus states the emotional reaction to the prior act.
segments.s664-g141: همی‌گفت is the iterative/past continuous 'kept saying' or 'would say'. کاکنون is a fused form of که اکنون 'that now' (که introducing the reported clause + اکنون 'now'). شود is the present/subjunctive form of 'شد' used optatively/hortatively here with future sense 'will be/shall be'. آگهی is 'news/information/word' and is the complement of شود: 'that now news will be (sent).'
segments.s664-g142: بدین is an old/dense expression meaning 'to this' (here written as ب + دین in the segmentation); it introduces the indirect object 'to this man'. ناجوانمرد is an adjective/noun meaning 'dishonourable / unmanly one' and بی‌فرهی is an adjectival phrase 'without refinement / uncultured'. Together they form the recipient phrase 'to this dishonourable, uncouth fellow.'
segments.s664-g143: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing reported content. موبد is the subject 'mōbed / the priest'. به زندان فرستاد is the verb phrase 'sent (to) prison' (به = to, زندان = prison, فرستاد = sent). چیز is the indefinite object 'something' — the whole clause reports 'that the mobad sent something to prison.'
segments.s664-g144: نیرزد is a present/past-narrative negative predicate meaning 'is not worth / does not deserve'. تن ما 'our body' is the grammatical subject (تن = body, ما = our/we). برش is a pronominal/indirect element meaning 'to him/for him' (not well represented by the provided senses here), and یک پشیز is a quantifier 'a single pashiz' (a trifle). The clause says 'our body is not worth even a single pashiz to him.'
segments.s664-g337: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' marking origin; it introduces the genitive noun phrase تشویر هرمز ('the counsel/advice of Hormoz'). فروپژمرید is an archaic past/stative verb meaning 'withered/declined' (the prefix فرو- is intensifying and the verb is in past tense). The phrase reads as a cause/result clause: 'from Hormoz's counsel he withered.'
segments.s664-g338: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آن modifies راست‌گفتار 'that truthful speech'. او را شنید is a simple past clause 'he heard him/his words' where را is the definite/object marker attached to او; شنید is the past tense of 'to hear'. The whole dependent clause gives the time: 'when he heard that truthful speech.'
segments.s664-g339: به زندان is a prepositional destination phrase 'into/ to prison' (به + noun). فرستادشان is the past verb 'sent' with the enclitic object pronoun -شان 'them' attached to the verb. تیره شب is an adverbial temporal/epithet 'on a dark/ominous night' placed after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s664-g340: وز here is an archaic/poetic connective (related to از) introducing what follows ('from/and from them'); ایشان is the pronoun 'they/them'. ببد is an archaic past copula/auxiliary meaning 'was' or 'became/turned'. تیز (an adjective) modifies the following verb phrase, and بگشاد (archaic past form with the verbal prefix ب-) means 'opened' (literally 'made open'). لب 'lip' is the noun object; the phrase metaphorically means 'opened a sharp lip' = 'spoke sharply.'
segments.s664-g341: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The clause uses vivid poetic imagery: 'سیمِ شب' ('the silver of night' = moonlight/night's silver) and برزد سر is an archaic verbal phrase meaning 'lifted/raised its head' (برزد is an older tense/form). از کوه ماه is a prepositional phrase 'from the mountain of the moon' indicating source/location. Word order follows poetic freedom but the temporal sense is 'when X rose from Y.'
segments.s664-g342: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. سیمای برزین is a genitive construction 'the visage/appearance of Barzin' (سیمای = 'face/visage', برزین a proper name). بپردخت is an archaic past verb meaning 'leapt forth/sprang' (here used of the king's sudden movement). شاه 'king' appears as the subject; the verb-noun ordering is idiomatic in epic poetry.
segments.s664-g343: به زندان is the destination phrase 'into the prison'. دزدان is the genitive/attributive noun 'of thieves' (i.e. 'the thieves' prison'). مر is an archaic datival pronoun 'to me' (meaning 'for me' or 'as far as I am concerned') and او را بکشت is an archaic perfect/past clause 'they killed him' where را marks او as definite object. The sequence says essentially 'they killed him in the thieves' prison (for me/as far as I'm concerned).'
segments.s664-g344: ندارد is present-tense third-person negative 'he/it does not have' or 'there is not (for him)'. جز ... means 'except'. از introduces the source 'from/except for' and رنج و نفرین list the things left: 'suffering and curse'. بمشت is an enclitic/dative phrase 'to me' (i.e. 'for me/as regards me'). Together: 'he has nothing for me except suffering and a curse.'
